title: Chapter 1: The Glimpse Within
text: The air in the outer sect disciple’s quarters was thin and carried the metallic tang of cheap spirit stones. Lin Jue sat cross-legged on his simple straw mat, the worn wood of the floor cool against his skin. His room was a cube of austerity: a bed, a small table, and a single, flickering oil lamp that cast long, dancing shadows against the paper-thin walls. Outside, the sounds of the Azure Stream Sect—the distant clang of steel on steel from the training grounds, the murmur of disciples returning from their chores—were a muted backdrop to the silence within. He was a ghost in the machine of the sect, one of thousands of outer disciples clinging to the promise of cultivation, a promise as thin as the morning mist on Azure Peak. For three years, he had been stuck at the third stage of Qi Condensation. Not a failure, but a monument to mediocrity. His talent was deemed average, his resources nonexistent. He was a stone in the river, unnoticed, unchanging, while others were swept along by the current of progress. This was the life of those who held the iron rice bowl of the outer sect: a life of bare subsistence, with the hope of immortality a distant, mocking star. Lin Jue closed his eyes. His breathing slowed, each inhale a measured pull of the world’s spiritual energy, each exhale a quiet release of mortal impurity. The qi, thin as it was in this neglected corner of the sect, trickled into his body, a sluggish stream seeping into parched earth. It followed the well-worn paths of the Azure Stream Foundation Art, circulating through his meridians, slowly gathering in the spiritual sea of his dantian. The process was frustratingly slow. He could feel the bottleneck, a dam of solidified impurities and spiritual resistance blocking his path to the fourth stage. Most disciples would attack such a barrier with brute force, spending months or even years gathering enough qi to shatter it in a single, desperate attempt. title: Chapter 2: Forest of Pursuit
text: Lin Jue slid the wooden door shut, the quiet click doing little to dispel the weight of the gaze that followed him like a second shadow. Xiyue’s eyes had not been those of a mere spectator. They had carried a chilling calculation, the kind that dissected a man’s soul in three breaths. He could not stay. Not with that kind of attention on him. He slipped out through the narrow, refuse-strewn alley behind the outer disciple quarters, his drab gray robes melting into the gathering dusk. The sect’s patrols, disciples seeking meager contribution points, would soon begin their evening rounds. Every step he took on the packed earth carried the echo of potential disaster. One wrong glance from an inner sect member, one idle question from a patrolling Senior Brother, and his life would unravel. He had no answers they would accept. The outer gates loomed ahead, a dark silhouette against the twilight sky, unmanned at this hour. With a practiced clumsiness honed over years of sneaking out for supplementary training, Lin Jue vaulted the low wall, landing without a whisper in the tall, dew-kissed grass beyond. Behind him, the Azure Cloud Sect’s thousand lanterns flickered to life, a constellation of false stars. Ahead lay the forbidden Beast Woods, a boundless sea of ancient, gnarled trees whose canopy swallowed even the faintest moonlight. He ran. Whipping branches, still damp from an afternoon shower, lashed at his face. His breathing remained a steady, controlled rhythm, a testament to sixteen years of discipline. Three hundred meters. Four hundred. His spiritual sense, limited by his meager realm, extended a paltry two hundred meters, but the Heaven-Glimpsing Pupil was bound by no such limit. He kept it half-closed, a narrow slit of incandescent violet hidden beneath his left eyelid, its gaze sweeping the terrain for the tell-tale glow of qi signatures. title: Chapter 3: Splintered Spear's Embrace
text: The roar of the cascading water was a constant, thunderous presence, masking the sound of Lin Jue’s ragged breathing. Moonlight, filtered through the shimmering curtain of the falls, cast a pale, ethereal glow upon the damp stone of the grotto. He lay propped against a smooth, moss-covered boulder, the chill of it seeping through the tattered remains of his outer sect disciple robes. A faint, whimpering sound drew his attention. Beside him, the small, fox-like spirit beast, Xiyue, nudged his hand with her nose. Her fur, the color of fresh snow, was matted with his blood. In the dim light, her violet eyes, a shade so deep they seemed to drink the darkness, were wide with a distress that mirrored the sigil pulsating faintly on her forehead. Lin Jue’s focus, however, was internal. His All-Seeing Eye, a gift of perception that transcended physical blindness, was turned inward, mapping the geography of his own ruin. A Blackwood Spear, its shaft splintered, was lodged in his right side, just below the ribcage. The head, a cruel triangle of enchanted iron, had pierced three cun deep. It had missed his vital organs by a hair’s breadth, a stroke of luck he could scarcely afford to celebrate. The problem was not the initial wound, but the aftermath. The spear was laced with a Qi-disrupting poison, a sluggish venom that was crawling through his meridians like frozen sludge, threatening to cripple his cultivation foundation permanently. His dantian, a sea of nascent qi that he had painstakingly filled to the peak of the third stage of Qi Condensation, was in turmoil. The venom was a foreign invader, and his own spiritual energy was fighting a losing war against it, depleting his reserves at an alarming rate. He had perhaps an hour before his foundation suffered irreparable damage. “It must come out,” he murmured, his voice a dry rasp. Xiyue yipped softly, her claws scrabbling at the stone floor. title: Chapter 4: Blind Eye, Seeing Heart
text: Lin Jue stepped through the weathered wooden arch of Ironwood Ford, and the clamor of the border town crashed against him like a tidal wave of unfiltered qi. The air was thick with the scent of roasted spirit-yams, cheap incense, and the faint, acrid tang of newly forged iron. Vendors hawked low-grade spirit herbs with voices raw from shouting, mortals haggled over iron talismans that promised more than they could deliver, and outer disciples from three minor sects pushed through the crowd with the unearned arrogance of big fish in a tiny pond. He kept his left eye squinted shut, his head tilted at an angle as if peering through a constant fog—the image of a half-blind outer sect waste, a role he had perfected over years of careful observation. His spiritual sense, however, was anything but blind. It extended four hundred and fifty meters in every direction, a silent, invisible net. The Heaven-Glimpsing Pupil, narrowed to a sliver of violet light beneath his eyelid, mapped every meridian pathway in the throng with chilling precision. He saw a portly merchant whose earth-rooted channels were clogged with greed-induced impurities, slowing his circulation by half. He saw a blacksmith whose fire meridians burned unevenly, wasting a full thirty percent of his daily qi with every swing of the hammer. Lin Jue absorbed each flaw, each imperfection, mirroring them against the flawless template of his own Nine Meridian Mirror to refine the divine technique further. A flicker of movement, too quick for a mortal eye. A hand, nimble as a striking viper, brushed against his waist. Slender fingers lifted the small leather pouch containing his remaining spirit stones and three low-grade pills. The thief was a ghost, melting back into the river of bodies without causing a single ripple. Jiang Qian. Lin Jue did not give chase. To do so would break character. title: Chapter 5: System Notification
text: Rain drummed a frantic rhythm against the paper windows of the Willow Shade Tea House, a sound like a thousand tiny fists. Inside, the air was thick and cloying, a mixture of cheap spirit tea, damp wood, and the sharp, metallic tang of fear-sweat. Lin Jue stood with his back to a splintered wooden table, his posture deceptively relaxed, left eye squinted in his well-practiced half-blind act. Across the room, Cao Shen blocked the only exit, a human wall of muscle and menace. The Verdant Brook Sect hunter’s aura pressed down like a physical weight, the crushing pressure of Qi Condensation Stage 7, a full three stages above him. It was a mountain-sea difference in raw power. “You blind waste from Azure Cloud,” Cao Shen snarled, the crack of his knuckles sharp in the tense silence. “Wang Long’s Spirit-Gathering Pill vanished the same day you and that thief girl appeared. Speak. Where is it?” Lin Jue kept his head lowered, a portrait of subservience. Yet beneath the meek exterior, his spiritual sense was a silent, sprawling net, extending six hundred meters in every direction. It mapped the tea house’s layout, the twelve customers frozen in their seats like statues, and most importantly, the precise, flawed flow of qi inside Cao Shen’s body. The Heaven-Glimpsing Pupil opened a mere sliver beneath his eyelid. The mundane world of color and form bled away, replaced by a stark reality of glowing spiritual energy. In this vision, Cao Shen was not a man but a construct of light, and his forearms were a disaster. They appeared as a tangled mess of jagged scars, not on his skin, but etched directly into his meridians—the telltale sign of the self-destructive Shattered Jade martial style. Each scar pulsed with a sickly, unstable light. The style burned the user’s own qi channels for bursts of explosive power, but the violent rebound created permanent, cascading weaknesses. title: Chapter 6: Violet Glow Cracks
text: Lin Jue sat cross-legged on the worn straw mat, the floorboards of the quiet inn room already thrumming with a low-frequency vibration. It was the rhythmic chanting of a tracking spell, a sound that pulsed through the wood like the heartbeat of an approaching predator. Jiang Qian pressed her back against the barred door, her battered short sword held in a white-knuckled grip. Her breathing was shallow, and sweat beaded on her brow despite the unnatural chill that had settled over the courtyard. The Core Formation elder was closing in. Lin Jue could feel the oppressive spiritual pressure pressing down from three li away. His own spiritual sense, now stable at the fourth stage of Qi Condensation, extended a full eight hundred meters, a bubble of awareness in the encroaching storm. He ignored the imminent danger. His dantian churned with restless qi, the crystalline blockages in his primary meridians pulsing like shards of frozen lightning. The Nine Meridian Mirror technique spun slowly in his sea of consciousness, a flawless silver disc reflecting every imperfection back at him with merciless clarity. “Brother Lin, that chanting… it’s getting stronger,” Jiang Qian whispered, her voice tight with strain. “I saw Senior Brother Wang Long circling the outer wall with his lackeys. We should run.” Lin Jue did not open his eyes. “Running without strength is pointless. The blockages must break first.” He turned the Heaven-Glimpsing Pupil inward. A flood of silent, violet light inundated his spiritual sea. The nine primary meridians appeared before his mind’s eye as glowing rivers, each one obstructed by jagged crystalline formations—the lingering poison of the old seal. The largest blockage sat at the critical intersection of his lung and heart meridians, a jagged mass measuring exactly 2.7 cun in diameter. title: Chapter 7: The Squinted Eye's Gaze
text: Chapter 7: The Squinted Eye’s Gaze Chapter 7 Chaos churned within the courtyard of the Ironwood Ford inn. A dreary afternoon sky cast a pallid light on the scene, reflected in the muddy puddles that splattered under stamping boots. At the center of it all stood Wang Long, his face a mask of rage. Earthen qi, thick and cloying, swirled around his right palm like a coiling serpent of ochre light. Across from him, Jiang Qian gripped a battered short sword, her slender body a shield for her two younger siblings huddled against a damp wooden wall. Her own qi, flickering faintly at the third stage of Qi Condensation, was a candle flame against his bonfire. Three steps behind her, Lin Jue stood with his left eye squinted in his practiced, clumsy-nearsighted guise. His spiritual sense, however, was anything but clumsy. Now expanded to a staggering two-thousand-meter radius since his breakthrough to Foundation Establishment, it painted every detail of the courtyard in his mind with crystalline clarity. Wang Long’s meridians glowed with glaring faults, a testament to a foundation built on pills rather than perseverance. The arrogant outer sect disciple’s elbow joint leaked a quantifiable twenty-three percent of his qi with every circulation. His stance, a sloppy imitation of a sect-standard martial form, favored his right leg by four degrees too much—a fatal imbalance born from unearned power. “You thief girl! You dared to touch my Spirit-Gathering Pill!” Wang Long’s bellow carried across the entire block, drawing more faces to the surrounding windows. “Hand it over, or by the heavens, I will break every bone in your body. And that blind waste hiding behind you will listen to you scream!” Jiang Qian’s knuckles whitened on her sword hilt, the worn leather groaning under the pressure. “This junior never touched Senior Brother Wang’s belongings. title: Chapter 8: Bloodline Memory Seal
text: Freezing rain lashed the outer sect grounds of the Azure Cloud Sect, each drop a needle of ice. A crystalline sheen coated every roof tile and blade of withered grass. Lin Jue pressed his back to the rough-hewn wood of the tool shed, the cold seeping through his thin robes. His spiritual sense was a taut web stretched to its limit of two thousand meters, a fragile thing under the crushing weight of the Core Formation elder’s aura pressing in from the east. From the south, the Verdant Brook hunters circled like wolves, their anti-pupil array humming with a discordant energy that grated against his senses. It was already eighty-three percent complete. The shed door, secured by a heavy iron lock, creaked open without a sound. A hooded figure slipped through the gap, bypassing every layer of his mirrored concealment talismans as if they were mere mist. Lin Jue’s hand, calloused and steady, tightened on the hilt of his battered short sword. His left eye, a secret he had guarded for sixteen years, opened a fraction. A sliver of profound violet light pierced the gloom. The figure lowered her hood. An old woman, her face a roadmap of wrinkles like dried tree bark, her eyes milky and clouded with age. Yet her spine was straight, her posture carrying the ghost of immense power. When the Heaven-Glimpsing Pupil focused, it saw past the frail facade to the truth within: her meridians were a tapestry of scars, wounds sustained in battles at the Nascent Soul level, at the very least. “You,” Lin Jue said, his voice flat and devoid of surprise. “You are not on any sect roster.” The old woman offered a smile, revealing teeth stained dark by decades of chewing bitter herbs. “Sixteen years I have waited for the seal to break, Young Master. Your mother, my lady, entrusted me with one final task.” Outside, thunder rolled, not the natural percussion of a storm, but a sound laced with the profound ice qi of the Core Formation elder. title: Chapter 9: The Blinded Eye's Static
text: Freezing rain continued to lash the outer sect grounds, turning the packed earth into a treacherous mire. With movements too swift for the eye to follow, the Core Formation elder slammed nine jade markers into the muddy ground. Each marker flared with a harsh, sterile white light, instantly forming the nine-sided anti-pupil array. A dome of shimmering energy snapped into existence, and the breathing geometry that had guided Lin Jue through the storm dissolved into chaotic, painful static. His left eye saw only noise. The strike team swarmed from three directions, their auras pressing down like collapsing mountains. Lin Jue’s spiritual sense still reached three thousand meters, a vast and lonely perception, but without the Heaven-Glimpsing Pupil, the world felt incomplete, a song with its melody stripped away. From the shadows, Xiyue lunged in her nine-tailed fox form. Her silver tails flickered, translucent and unstable, the talisman fracture on her flank pulsing with a sickly light. She moved with a pained slowness, her usual grace stolen by the array. Lin Jue acted without hesitation. He grabbed her by the scruff of her neck and dragged her behind the half-collapsed wall of a tool shed. His steps followed the exact spatial map he had burned into his memory one breath before the array activated. Mud, thick and cold, sucked at his worn boots. The freezing rain soaked through his patched gray robes, clinging to his skin like a second, icy layer. “Hold on,” he said, his voice a low command. His dantian circulated the Nine Meridian Mirror technique at its absolute limit. Qi flowed through his twelve main meridians in perfect, mirrored cycles, but the absence of visual guidance made every circulation feel heavy, like dragging chains through sand. The Core Formation elder laughed, a sound that rolled across the outer sect like thunder splitting the sky. “Blind at last, little seer. title: Chapter 10: Lin Jue stood motionless within the heart of the anti-pupil
text: Lin Jue stood motionless within the heart of the anti-pupil array. The nine-sided formation hummed with a malignant power, its one hundred and eight spirit stones pulsing in a malevolent, synchronized rhythm. A torrent of white noise flooded his left eye, a chaotic static that drowned out all sight. The Heaven-Glimpsing Pupil saw only formless chaos. His spiritual sense, a razor-fine net of perception, extended to its absolute limit of four thousand meters, yet the Core Formation elder’s aura pressed down upon him like an iron mountain, heavy and suffocating. The elder’s laughter echoed through the curtains of freezing rain, a sound dripping with contempt. “Blind at last, you worthless seer. Without your forbidden eye, you are less than an ant crawling in the mud.” Lin Jue kept his left eye shut. It was useless now. But the breathing geometry, the fundamental rhythm of the world his pupil had shown him, remained etched into his very bones. It was a faint, persistent pulse that he could now match to the elder’s own heartbeat. Every subtle shift in the man’s weight sent measurable vibrations through the slick mud. The old injury in the elder’s left knee, a structural weakness of precisely eleven percent, announced itself with every flicker of intent. The probability of a direct killing strike in the next three breaths of time sat at seventy-eight percent. Pressed against his side, Xiyue’s small fox form trembled. Her silver fur was matted with rain and blood, a stark contrast to the sickly violet light pulsing from the Reverse-Meridian Soul-Trap branded on her flank. Their blood-oath contract, a bond forged in desperation, transmitted the searing agony of the seal directly into his meridians. He partitioned the pain, filed it away, and ignored it. title: Chapter 1: The Unblinking Gaze
text: In Room 309 the privacy curtain moved in air that was not moving. Three doors down the red sharps bin clicked once, a settling sound plastic does not make on its own. Near the fire escape something small and metallic fell from a shelf that had nothing on it. The ballast in the ceiling fixture held a dissonant note just above hearing. Jin Shiyu leaned on the mop handle and tried to remember if Lin Wu had left the kitchen lamp on for him. Third week on the job. He had been told to expect the sounds and to treat them as he treated the smell of bleach: something the walls did, not something to explain. The dayroom floor had to be mopped and buffed before shift change. He worked it in overlapping arcs, shoulders beginning to complain. The polyester of the uniform stuck between his shoulder blades. Disinfectant coated the back of his throat, that raw chemical sharpness you never quite got used to, and underneath it the building’s own older smell: stale linen, something damp in the vents. In the corner by the sealed window, Patient Huang sat in the molded plastic chair. He often stayed there past midnight, looking out at the grounds. Tonight his stillness had a different quality. Jin Shiyu set the mop in the bucket and listened. The thing missing was easy to name once he thought to look for it: the soft wet rhythm of a sleeping man’s breath. He crossed the room. Two fingers to the radial artery. The wrist was cool, a cold he had only felt on the inside of a dishwasher door in winter. He straightened up to walk to the wall phone and found he couldn’t, quite, because Patient Huang’s eyes were open and fixed on him. He stepped left. The gaze came with him. He took a full step right. It tracked. The pupils were dilated and reflected the long white line of the fluorescent. Jin Shiyu leveled his voice the way he had learned to in university seminars when an interviewer wanted to rattle him. He lifted the receiver. “Patient Huang in the dayroom. title: Chapter 2: Seven Breaths Hold
text: “You saw him.” Old Wei said it without turning, his back ramrod-straight on the edge of the cot. “The one in the chair. I can tell. You are standing straight.” Jin Shiyu, halfway through swapping the trash liner, did not break the motion. The black plastic crinkled in the small room. “Most of the new ones shake. When they lift a body that watches. You didn’t. Good. Next time, count inside your chest. Hold it steady.” He twisted the neck of the full bag, knotted it twice, and set it beside the door. The knot was tight. The plastic was thin. Neither response was expected. The shift dragged on in its particulars. Paper cups to the dispenser at the second-floor station. The water cooler, refilled from a heavy jug, gurgling into its base. By the time he had emptied three sharps bins, glass on plastic on metal, the familiar unsettling weight of other people’s waste, his watch was past two. He locked fresh ones into place. The smell of disinfectant clung to his uniform and failed to cover the building’s deeper odors. At three the hospital settled. The ventilators and monitors merged into a single low note, like a car horn in a tunnel becoming the tunnel’s sound. In the dayroom Old Wei was waiting. He had taken the molded plastic chair Huang had occupied, his hands flat on his knees. His stillness had the absolute quality of a photograph. “Come here, boy.” Jin Shiyu approached in his rubber-soled silence. His awareness stayed contained, a small sphere around him, narrower than the room itself. Old Wei gestured to the chair opposite. The plastic was cool against the backs of his legs. “Seven breaths. That is the first hold. Inhale through the nose. Count one. Hold. Count two. Release through the mouth. Count three. Do not let the seam slip. If it slips, your throat closes.” Jin Shiyu listened. title: Chapter 3: The Red Silk Ribbon
text: A patient stood barefoot in the long-stay corridor, her paper slippers curled at the edges three meters back where she had stepped out of them. Her shoulders shook. A paper cup lay overturned at her feet, and the tablets it had held were rolling into the dark grout lines between the tiles, finding them the way water finds a crack. A nurse approached with measured steps, rubber soles whispering. The patient saw her, pressed her back flat against the wall, and slid down until she was seated on the cold linoleum. Gu Suyan emerged from the dayroom. She wore the standard striped gown. Her gait was stiff with the new medication schedule, knees and hips working through something, and in her hands she cupped her own paper allotment of pills. A small green apple sat balanced on the rim. She stopped beside the crying woman and took the apple and placed it in the woman’s open trembling palm, and said nothing. The woman stared at the fruit. Her sobs quieted to hiccups that went ragged and then ordinary. The nurse took her by the arm. This time the arm came. Jin Shiyu pushed the mop bucket forward. He kept his face empty. The calm had arrived too cleanly, he thought. An apple could not do that, not on its own. The mop left a dark glistening trail that smelled of bleach. Sunday morning came as a thin gray wash through the apartment window. He sat at the formica table eating cold rice and stir-fried vegetables. Lin Wu had left for her uncle’s auto parts shop an hour before. On the rug, Jin Yue stacked her blocks into uneven towers. The wood clicked together in a dry small rhythm. A knock at the door. Three measured taps. His mother stood in the hallway. A plain gray coat buttoned to the neck despite the June warmth. The lines around her eyes had multiplied, fine as cracks in plaster. She stayed on the landing. No word about the job, the wife, the granddaughter. She extended a hand. A thin red silk ribbon lay across her palm. title: Chapter 4: Silent Fissure
text: His fourth week on nights and Jin Shiyu was leaning on the mop when patient Feng collapsed. Earlier in the shift a child’s red rubber ball had rolled from beneath a bed in a room that had been empty three days, crossed the hall, and come to a silent stop against the opposite wall. He had watched the ball settle and kept mopping. Feng’s body hit the floor with a dull dense impact, the sound of a dropped sandbag. His chest heaved once, spastic, and a fissure tore itself open across his torso, starting at the sternum, branching toward the ribs, the edges black and sharp as torn construction paper. It widened perhaps two centimeters. No blood came out. A faint sterile smell. Jin Shiyu kept his awareness pulled in, small, the size of a thought. The fissure’s edges fluttered a subtle dry movement against the fabric of the gown. Charge Nurse Chen reached him first. Her soles made no sound. She knelt. Her fingers went to Feng’s neck. “Code blue, Room 2 corridor.” Flat, devoid of alarm. Two other orderlies arrived with a gurney. They rolled Feng onto the vinyl. By the time they had the straps done up, the fissure had sealed itself from the outside. The skin was slack but unbroken. Heart failure, the report would state. He pushed the mop bucket closer. It left wet parallel tracks. He worked around the spot where Feng had fallen, the cotton absorbing a patch of darkness from the tile. He dunked the mop and wrung it twice. Gray fibers dripped murky water back into the pail. Elder Bai was waiting in the sterile shadow of the supply closet. The metal door was ajar a hand’s breadth. He wore a pale blue maintenance uniform of an older cut, soft at the collar and cuffs. A broom in one hand. He waited for Jin Shiyu to step over the threshold and the door to click shut. “Look at the body again,” Elder Bai said. The voice had no volume yet filled the small windowless space, pressing in from the shelves. “Relax your focus. Count of seven. From the diaphragm.” He inhaled through his nose

title: Chapter 5: Syllables in Shadow
text: Suyan was in the doorway when he came down the corridor. Bare feet on the cold floor. Striped gown loose on her thin frame. A fine tremor running through her shoulders. The fluorescent tube above her hummed an irregular pulse, flickering once every seven seconds. Jin Shiyu stopped his mop bucket five meters down the corridor. The gray water sloshed once and settled. He kept his expression neutral. He pushed the bucket forward. The plastic wheels rolled without sound on freshly mopped linoleum. Suyan kept her eyes on the tile until he reached the threshold. They came up sharp. She spoke first, quiet, pitched beneath the microphone. “You told me your full name on Tuesday. In the dayroom. I wasn’t paying attention.” She paused. Her fingers twitched at her sides. “I woke up an hour ago. A character kept turning in my head. Your family name. I checked the restricted intake directory. No one has that name here. Tonight. Please.” He did not move. He had no reason to agree. Lin Wu waited at home. Jin Yue would wake at six. The words left him before he could weigh them. “Alright.” Suyan exhaled. The sound was shallow, a rustle. She stepped fully into the bathroom. Her feet made no noise. He remained in the doorway and pressed his left thumb against the small crescent at its base. The pressure was ordinary. The mark carried no heat, no pulse. She positioned herself before the large mirror above the sinks. The light flickered. Her reflection stared back, pale. The chrome cup dispenser at the sink’s edge caught a distorted sliver of the corridor behind her, a gray patch of his shoulder, nothing more. “Speak it,” she said, flat, directed at the mirror. “The full family name. Three characters. I need the exact tone.” He gave it to her without inflection. Jin. The second character. The third. title: Chapter 6: Wooden Block Tower
text: “Lǜ-lǜ,” Jin Yue said again, holding the slice of cucumber between two small fingers as if it might escape. Juice ran down her chin. Jin Shiyu watched her chew. The shao bing on her plate was untouched. The morning’s roasted sesame and grease and soy milk filled the air around the vendor’s stall. Lin Wu broke a piece of shao bing. The flaky layers separated with a crisp sound. She laughed, a short warm puff of air. “Yueyue, the cucumber isn’t the only thing to eat. Have some bun, like Baba.” Jin Yue’s round face was grave. She took another precise bite of the slice. The cucumber was the pale green it had always been. He lifted his cup. The soy milk was lukewarm. The stall’s metal countertop reflected the street behind him without distortion. No additional figures. He drank. The taste was ordinary. Lin Wu reached across the table and wiped Yueyue’s chin with a napkin. Her shoulder-length hair, tied back with a simple band, shifted with the motion. “She’s been like this since she woke up. Only the cucumber. No congee, no bun. Just lǜ-lǜ .” He nodded. His hand rested on the table, the crescent mark at the base of his left thumb pressed against textured plastic. The memory of the bathroom two nights ago sat on him: Suyan’s pale face after the lights returned, the flat denial, the tremor she had been hiding badly. He pushed the memory down. The stall’s awning cast a clean shadow. A bicycle bell rang twice. They finished and Lin Wu paid with worn bills. Jin Yue clutched the last half-slice in one fist and Tutu in the other. They walked the short distance to the building. The concrete steps felt solid. No pressure from below. Inside, the wooden blocks were scattered on the living room rug. He lowered himself to the floor, his civilian trousers stretching at the knees. Jin Yue sat opposite. She placed a block painted with 木. Then another. Her small fingers moved with deliberate care. title: Chapter 7: Tutu in the Hallway
text: Tutu in the hallway. He would think it later, on the bedroom threshold, with the half-turned valve in his palm. He thought it three hours early, mopping the vinyl in 412, and could not say where the thought had come from. He kept mopping. Charge Nurse Chen passed in the deep quiet before three. He emptied the sharps bin in 412 and sealed the hinge. The contents shifted, glass on plastic on metal. He wiped the counter with a bleach-soaked cloth. Disinfectant laid itself over the building’s permanent odor of illness and covered nothing. He reached the third-floor women’s corridor at five past three. 309’s door was closed, curtain drawn tight. The women’s bathroom door was closed too; he left it. The black mirror inside could wait. The birthmark at the base of his left thumb itched once and stopped. Old Wei was in the far dayroom chair, folding a scrap of newspaper into smaller and smaller squares. “The valve under the sink,” Old Wei said, to his hands. “Remember to check it when you get home.” Jin Shiyu kept the mop moving. The bucket’s wheels left parallel wet tracks. The old man seemed to want no answer. He signed the night log at seven. The clipboard went to Chen without comment. The morning light outside was the color of dirty water. In the basement he put the uniform down the chute and changed into his civilian shirt. On its collar, high up near the inside seam, a faint sweet smell clung, metallic under the soap. It passed him without registering. The subway was long. The car cleared a small perimeter around his tired face as it always did. His reflection slid across the dark window and matched him, stride for stride. He climbed the three flights into the early morning. Halfway up he knew something. The shape of the knowing was ahead of the words. At his door the air had changed. title: Chapter 8: The Dissonant Hum
text: Jin Shiyu sat on a folding metal chair. The starch in his borrowed shirt held the sleeves a few millimeters off his wrists. The birthmark at the base of his left thumb pulsed once, a slow deep ache that synchronized with the hum from the fluorescent tubes overhead. Two photographs stood side by side on a low table draped in white polyester. The incense burner trailed an oily thread that refused to disperse. A funeral director in a dark synthetic suit adjusted a frame that did not need adjusting. The lilies had begun to turn in the heat. His mother arrived late in the morning. She moved with the brittle caution of someone expecting the floor to give way. Her graying hair absorbed the weak light. Dark clothes hung on her. She looked ten years older than she had three weeks ago. She took the chair beside him without a word. The air around the two chairs went thin and cold, a small vacuum in the larger quiet of the hall. He kept his eyes on the photographs. Lin Wu’s practical smile, from their last trip to the botanical gardens. Jin Yue’s round face and short pigtails, Tutu clutched in one arm. The angle hid the mark on her thumb. He had chosen it himself. The director returned around noon with a low practiced murmur about the interment schedule. His mother gave a single sharp nod. Jin Shiyu signed the form. Ballpoint pen, clean unbroken stroke of black. The director collected the clipboard and left them alone again. At the interment the sky was a flat uniform gray. As the urns were lowered, Jin Liwen reached across the small space between them. Her hand, dry and cool, closed over his left. Her fingers locked onto his thumb with a strength that felt like bone grinding on bone. The pressure was precise, centered on the birthmark. She held it a full minute, her gaze fixed on the open grave. She had not held his thumb that way at his wedding, or only briefly, the once, before letting go. He left his hand where she had taken it. title: Chapter 9: Gas Leak
text: “My wife and daughter. Two weeks ago. Same model of valve. Same phrasing.” Jin Shiyu was rehearsing the sentence inside his chest as he pushed the mop bucket down the third-floor corridor, his body committed to the task while the rest of him drifted somewhere a floor or two above. Gray water sloshed once against the bucket’s side and settled. He had not yet decided whether to say it aloud. Chen Hua passed at three without speaking, her soft soles leaving faint damp prints that evaporated in seconds. He collected the sharps bin from 411. The yellow plastic container clicked shut. He wiped the counter with a bleach-soaked cloth. The chemical smell layered over the permanent hospital odor, changing nothing. Just past three he reached the women’s corridor. 309’s door was closed; the privacy curtain behind its narrow window was drawn tight, no silhouette. Fifty meters down, the bathroom door stood ajar. A blade of white light cut across the polished floor. He parked the bucket against the wall. The wet mop head dripped onto the vinyl. He walked toward the light. His rubber soles made no sound. Gu Suyan was inside, barefoot before the mirror, nineteen, the fitted striped gown clinging to her waist and ending high on her thighs, her hair shoulder-length and blunt-cut, falling straight over the pale slender column of her neck. Her eyes, dark and almond-shaped, held the flat heavy glaze of her medication. Her shoulders stayed angled toward the glass when his footsteps stopped in the doorway. He remained on the threshold, posture unchanged from the night of the ritual. The gray fabric of the uniform felt stiff on his shoulders. The birthmark on his left thumb pulsed its low steady ache. He spoke without raising his voice. The ceiling microphone was live. “Your parents. Gas leak. The report ruled it accidental.” Her posture froze. Her bare feet stayed flat on the cool tile. Her hands, hanging loose, curled into fists. She had lost weight in the three weeks since the blackout.

title: Chapter 10: The Empty Bed
text: Three days. The number turned over and over inside his chest as he pushed the mop down the third-floor corridor. Gu Suyan had been here three days ago. The handle of the bucket vibrated faintly in his grip. Gray water, smelling of dust and bleach, sloshed against the plastic sides. Chen Hua passed at three, head down. Her crepe-soled shoes made no sound but left faint damp prints on the freshly mopped linoleum. He unlocked the cabinet in Room 411 and retrieved the full sharps bin. Heavier than usual. The plastic needles and glass vials inside shifted with a dry rattle. He carried it to the disposal chute, slid it in, pulled the lever. The heavy thud echoed from below. He returned, snapped a new yellow bin into place, wiped the counter with a bleach cloth. Just past three he reached the women’s corridor. The door to Room 309 stood open. Its magnetic lock was disengaged. The privacy curtain had been pulled all the way back on its track; he heard in memory the small clatter of the plastic hooks scraping along the metal. The bed was stripped to the bare mattress, dark green vinyl stretched tight over foam. A faint body-shaped depression remained in its center. No pillow. No sheets. The small metal locker beside the bed hung open, its door angled a few centimeters from the frame. Her blue-and-white striped gown was gone. Her paper cup, the one with the symbol scratched into the wax with a fingernail, was gone from the nightstand. The room smelled only of industrial disinfectant. He parked the bucket, its wheel chocked against the wall. The wet gray mop head dripped a slow rhythmic patter. He stepped inside. His shoes were silent on the cold linoleum. The birthmark, a deep crimson map under the skin, pulsed once with a dull familiar ache. He scanned the space. Nothing answered. The room was a room. Empty. Cleaned. title: Chapter 11: Black Mirror's Echo
text: Old Wei sat in the corner chair when Jin Shiyu came back from the linen closet, upright in the standard blue-and-white striped gown, gray hair a thin haze under the humming light, one hand palm-down on the plastic armrest, the other clutching an envelope. The patient on the cot across the dayroom had turned his face to the wall. From somewhere down the corridor, a soft impact, nothing on the shelf. He pushed the mop bucket past the last row of vinyl chairs. The gray water inside reflected the ceiling panels in broken wavering lines. He stopped the bucket. The wheels squeaked once on the polished floor. He placed two fingers on the back of Old Wei’s resting hand. The skin was warm. He checked the carotid artery. No pulse. The envelope was addressed in neat precise characters to Madam Jin Liwen . His mother’s full name. He had never heard it spoken inside these walls. He slipped the envelope into the breast pocket of his gray orderly uniform. The birthmark at the base of his left thumb gave a single hard pulse. He ran the count inside his chest. In. Hold. Out. The body would stay in the chair. He would report it at seven. The corridor accepted the silence. He continued his rounds. The mop head slapped against the vinyl. Gray water dripped from the cotton strings. Later in the shift he emptied the last trash bin in the women’s corridor. 309 was stripped. The bed showed only the dark green vinyl of the mattress cover. The metal locker door hung open by three centimeters. No gown. No paper cup. No trace. The bathroom door at the end of the hall was closed. The absolute black mirror waited behind it. His hand stayed off the handle. A low hum vibrated up through the soles of his shoes, a frequency that matched the dull ache in his thumb. He returned to the dayroom. The body remained where it had been. The charge nurse had not come through. He sat in the vinyl chair opposite Old Wei. The envelope felt heavy in his pocket. title: Chapter 1: The Bite at Bahía Anasco
text: It had been four hours since the helicopter set down on the sand below the clinic. Roberta Carter washed her hands a second time. The faucet at the Bahía Anasco clinic ran hot or cold by no rule that Roberta Carter had been able to determine in her seven months of practice, and tonight the water ran hot, and the soap she lifted from the dish was the gritty pumice soap the orderlies bought in five-kilo blocks at the market in Puntarenas. She rinsed. She dried on a towel that smelled of bleach. She put on a fresh pair of gloves. She turned to the table. The man on the table was a construction worker from the InGen site on the island. He had been brought across by the night helicopter, on a board, with one of the InGen company doctors next to him pumping a green saline bag with two hands. The InGen company doctor had stayed long enough to fill out a single-page admit form in English and to leave on the desk, in cash, an amount of US dollars that Roberta Carter, who had spent half a year arguing with the regional health office over a single generator, knew the value of exactly. The man in the InGen field jacket had then walked back down the path to the beach, and the helicopter had lifted, and the helicopter had not come back. The patient had a name. Roberta Carter did not have it. The admit form said, in the Patient Name line, Manuel R. and then a question mark. The wound was not a wound, in the sense Roberta Carter had been trained to think of wounds. It was an absence. A hand-sized portion of the man’s left flank, beginning at the base of the lowest rib and running down past the hip, was simply not there. The edge of the absence was clean as if a knife had taken it, ragged as if a row of dull teeth had taken it, scorched as if a heat had taken it, and Roberta Carter, who was a competent emergency physician, could not for the first half-minute decide which of those three things had happened. The patient was still alive. He opened his eyes. He looked at Roberta Carter

title: Chapter 2: The Lizard at Cabo Blanco
text: It had been a hundred and forty miles since Mike Bowman had said the word paradise to his wife in a way that meant he was tired of saying it. Mike Bowman, fifty-one, of Highland Park, Dallas, had taken his wife Ellen and their daughter Tina on a Christmas drive down the Pan-American Highway from San José to the Pacific coast on the recommendation of a man at the rental counter who had said the word paradise to him with what Mike Bowman had taken, at the time, to be sincerity. The man had also said the word Cabo Blanco , and the word biological reserve , and had drawn a small careful map on the back of the rental form. Mike Bowman had now followed the map for two days. The map had taken them past Quepos and through a banana plantation and across a one-lane bridge and down a logging road that became a footpath that became, at the very end, a wide stretch of empty sand at the edge of a green headland with no buildings on it, no boats in the water, and, by the standards of Highland Park, no one. Tina Bowman, eight years old, kicked off her sandals and ran for the surf. “Tina.” “Yes, Daddy.” “Watch where you’re going.” “Yes, Daddy.” Ellen Bowman, who had been a hairdresser before she had been a wife and was now neither, set the cooler down on the towel and looked at her husband across the sand. Mike Bowman was looking down the beach. Ellen Bowman, who had been married to Mike Bowman for twelve years, knew the look. It was the look of a man who had paid a great deal of money for a thing and had been told the thing was worth it, and was now standing in the thing trying to decide whether the thing was worth it. “It’s beautiful,” Ellen Bowman said. “It’s empty.” “That’s why it’s beautiful.” “That’s why it’s empty.” Tina Bowman, halfway to the water, stopped and crouched. Tina Bowman picked something up. Tina Bowman held it up to the morning sun. “Mommy,” Tina Bowman called. Ellen Bowman looked up. Tina Bowman was holding a small piece of something dark in her palm. title: Chapter 3: A Drawing in Crayon
text: It had been two weeks since the lizard at Cabo Blanco when Dr. Richard Stone, of the Columbia University Tropical Diseases Laboratory, opened the manila envelope from the Clínica Santa María in Puntarenas, Costa Rica. The envelope contained, by the customs declaration on the front of it, one (1) frozen tissue sample, biological , and inside it, in a small heat-sealed plastic pouch, was the saliva and the broken skin of an animal that had bitten an eight-year-old American girl on a beach south of Quepos. The bite had been treated. The girl had recovered. The Costa Rican attending physician at the clinic had, in a careful covering letter, asked Dr. Stone if he could identify the saliva. The Costa Rican physician suspected, by the report of the parents, that the animal had been a kind of basilisk lizard, of which there were several species in the region, but the parents had insisted that the animal had walked on two legs, and the Costa Rican physician, who had been a Costa Rican physician for twenty-six years and had never heard of a two-legged basilisk, was, in his careful English, desirous of confirmation. Richard Stone read the covering letter twice. He set the pouch on the desk. He picked up the office phone and called Alice Levin. Alice Levin came into Richard Stone’s office two minutes later. Alice Levin was thirty-one and was the only research associate in the Tropical Diseases Lab who had ever worked in the field, in her case three summers in the Petén jungle in Guatemala, and Richard Stone, who had not, called Alice Levin in whenever a thing came across his desk that smelled of mud. “What.” Richard Stone pushed the pouch toward her. “Costa Rica. Bitten an American kid.” Alice Levin held the pouch up to the fluorescent. She looked at the small dried smear of saliva on the inside of the plastic. She looked at the small flake of pale skin in one corner. “Send it to virology.” “For what.” “Whatever it bit her with. They will run a panel.” “They have run the panel.

title: Chapter 4: The Dart and the Howler
text: It had been nine days since Alice Levin had said Procompsognathus in Richard Stone’s office in New York when Marty Guitierrez, of the Carara Biological Station on the Costa Rican mainland, took the dart pistol out of the back of his Land Rover and began to walk in a slow careful arc through the trees toward the howler monkey that was, in the high crook of a kapok tree forty meters off, eating something. Marty Guitierrez was forty-four. Marty Guitierrez had been at the Carara Biological Station for nine years. Marty Guitierrez had, in those nine years, darted approximately three hundred and sixty howlers, four hundred and forty white-faced capuchins, two squirrel monkeys, and one ocelot, of which the ocelot had been an accident, and Marty Guitierrez was, by the slow counting of his colleagues, the best dart-pistol shot at the station and possibly on the western coast of the country. The howler in the kapok was a young male. The young male howler was eating a small striped animal. The small striped animal was, from forty meters and through a gap in the canopy that opened and closed as the wind moved the upper leaves, hard to identify. Marty Guitierrez had brought the field glasses up twice. Through the field glasses he had been able to see, in the howler’s clever black hands, an animal about the size of a small rat, pale green and brown, with a long delicate tail, four limbs, and a row of small dark teeth. The howler had been eating the head first. Marty Guitierrez did not know the species. title: Chapter 5: The Old Man's Pitch
text: It had been three days since the call from Marty Guitierrez when John Hammond, of InGen Corporation, walked into the thirty-fourth-floor conference room of Cowan, Swain & Ross at One Embarcadero in San Francisco, set a small portable cage on the polished walnut table, lifted the cloth from the cage with a small theatrical flourish that Daniel Ross had been waiting all morning for, and asked the room to please hold the questions until the end. In the cage, on a bed of straw, was an elephant. The elephant was nine inches tall. The elephant raised its trunk and made a small annoyed sound that was, by the strict standards of the elephant’s larger relatives in the African savanna, a perfect trumpet, only smaller. Then the elephant walked, in the deliberate four-step rolling gait of its larger relatives, to the side of the cage, hooked its trunk around a single peanut that John Hammond had placed there for it, lifted the peanut to its mouth, and ate the peanut. In the conference room, three of the four lawyers laughed. The fourth lawyer was Daniel Ross. Daniel Ross, sixty-one, the senior partner at Cowan, Swain & Ross and the lead counsel for InGen Corporation since 1982, did not laugh. “John,” Daniel Ross said. “Daniel.” “Sit down. Tell me what you have done.” John Hammond, seventy-six, of London and Palo Alto and a private island off the Costa Rican coast that the people in this room did not, with the exception of Daniel Ross, know about, sat down. He folded his hands on the table. He let his eyes go from the cage to Daniel Ross’s face and back to the cage. He did not, on the whole, look like a man who had done a thing. title: Chapter 6: The Other Bidder
text: It was raining on Cupertino the morning the Biosyn board of directors voted, by a show of hands, to steal what John Hammond had built. The Biosyn boardroom was a room that had been designed by a man who had been told to design a boardroom. It had a long glass table. It had six high-backed chairs in black leather. It had a small pitcher of water in the center of the table and six glasses around it. It had, on the wall opposite the windows, a flat-screen television that, on this Tuesday morning, was showing a satellite map of an island off the Costa Rican coast that did not, according to all of the publicly available marine charts, exist. There were five men in the room. The sixth chair, at the head of the table, was empty. The five men were waiting for the man in the straw hat. Lewis Dodgson, forty-eight, of Saskatchewan and Stanford and a small white house in Cupertino with a pool he had never used, came in through the side door at four minutes after nine. He set the hat on the table. He set the folder beside the hat. He looked at the men. “We have it,” Lewis Dodgson said. “All of it,” said one of the men. “All of it. The man at the airport. title: Chapter 7: The Shaving-Cream Can
text: It had been six days since the Biosyn boardroom when Dennis Nedry, of San Mateo, walked across the Saturday-morning food court of San Francisco International Airport at twenty minutes after eight in the morning, bought a cup of coffee at the kiosk between the bagel place and the pretzel place, sat down at the small Formica two-top in the corner of the food court that he had been told to sit down at, set his cup of coffee on the table, opened the in-flight-magazine he had been told to open, and waited for the man in the straw hat. Dennis Nedry was, in every observable particular at eight-twenty-three in the morning at the San Francisco airport, a man on his way to a long lunch. Dennis Nedry was, by the small careful interior accounting Dennis Nedry was doing in the back of his head while he turned the pages of the magazine, a man with a heart rate of one hundred and ten beats per minute and rising. He had been at the airport for forty minutes. He had checked his bag at the curb. title: Chapter 8: The Snakewater Eggshell
text: It had been five weeks since the start of the Snakewater season when Alan Grant set his palm flat against the warm shaly hillside and felt, through the rock, the muffled thump of the sonic device the students up the slope called Thumper. Alan Grant’s palm was the palm of a paleontologist who had spent twenty summers on the badlands of eastern Montana, and the back of the palm was burnt the deep red-brown that the back of a paleontologist’s palm became after twenty summers, and the inside of the palm was pale, and the inside of the palm carried, that morning in the eighth week of June 1989, a fine grit of ferruginous siltstone that Alan Grant had not, in the course of brushing the morning’s overburden out of the test pit, bothered to wash off. Alan Grant lifted the palm. He looked at the rock. He listened. The thump came again. A row of yellow contour lines walked across the screen of the battery-powered portable computer that the students had set up on a beer carton at the top of Hill Four. The screen ghosted. The screen blanked. “Lost the integrator input,” one of the students said. “Get it back,” Alan Grant said. Alan Grant did not look at the student. Alan Grant looked at Ellie Sattler, who was thirty meters down the slope, kneeling beside a square of opened earth that, three days earlier, had been simply a square of earth, and that, today, contained a partial lateral exposure of the smallest skeleton Alan Grant had pulled out of the Snakewater badlands in a decade of digging there. Ellie Sattler did not look up. title: Chapter 9: The Helicopter Through the Cloud
text: It had been nineteen hours since the rotor wash off the InGen Bell JetRanger had flattened the bunchgrass at the Choteau airstrip when Alan Grant, of the University of Denver, fastened the four-point harness over the chest of his field shirt in the rear cabin of a second helicopter, this one a larger civilian Sikorsky in white-and-blue InGen livery, parked on the apron of Juan Santamaría International Airport outside the Costa Rican capital, and listened, through the inadequate gray foam of the headset clamped over his ears, to the pilot of the second helicopter run his pre-flight cross-talk with the tower in San José. Alan Grant’s harness was twisted at the left shoulder. Alan Grant’s headset cushion smelled faintly of another person’s hair oil. Alan Grant’s coffee cup, which Alan Grant had set in the small rubberized cup-holder built into the bulkhead beside his right knee, contained, at this point, only the bitter quarter-inch of grit that the second cup of the morning always contained when Alan Grant had been awake since four. title: Chapter 10: Welcome to Jurassic Park
text: It had been forty minutes since the trumpeting of the apatosaurs above the palms when Ellie Sattler, of the University of Denver, sat down in a leather seat in the small auditorium of the Isla Nublar visitor center and felt, in the cool conditioned air on the back of her neck, the small unlocatable shiver of a person who had walked, in the space of one short morning, out of her professional life and into a building that had been designed by the same firm of architects that did the corporate campuses in Palo Alto. Ellie Sattler’s thigh was sore. Ellie Sattler’s left palm carried the dry chalk of the rough timber handrail Ellie Sattler had used to come down the wet path. Ellie Sattler’s field journal was, at this moment, closed on her lap, with a black ballpoint clipped to the spine and the right thumb of Ellie Sattler holding the place she had stopped writing two minutes earlier, when Ed Regis had walked the group across the polished marble floor of the entrance hall, under a rotunda of pale skylit beams, past a banner that read WHEN DINOSAURS RULED THE EARTH, into the small auditorium for what Ed Regis had called, in his cheerful rehearsed voice, the orientation video. The auditorium had thirty-six seats and held five. The lights went down. The screen lit up in the soft blue of corporate stock animation. A voice that was not John Hammond’s voice but that was, by every careful intonation, the voice of a man who had been hired to sound exactly like John Hammond’s voice, began to speak about the cretaceous, the Mesozoic, the slow inexorable arc of the great extinction, the recovery of trace DNA from biting insects preserved for a hundred million years in golden Caribbean amber. Alan Grant, in the seat to Ellie Sattler’s left, did not laugh. title: Chapter 11: Two Hundred and Thirty-Eight
text: It had been twenty minutes since the infant Velociraptor had blinked once at Ellie Sattler in the nursery when Donald Gennaro, of Cowan, Swain & Ross, stepped through the security door of the Isla Nublar control room and, for the first time since the Sikorsky had set down on the helipad in the cloud, allowed himself to feel, in the small private space behind his sternum, the precise measure of how much money was in this room. Donald Gennaro’s right palm carried the chalk smear of his own anxious handprint from the hatchery rail. Donald Gennaro’s coffee was cold in the paper cup the cafeteria woman had pressed on him at the door. Donald Gennaro’s necktie, which Donald Gennaro had loosened in the helicopter and re-tied in the visitor-center men’s room and loosened again in the auditorium and forgotten about in the nursery, hung at his collar in the small embarrassing way it hung at the collar of a man who had stopped paying attention to his own appearance several hours earlier. Two banks of consoles ran the long axis of the room, and at the consoles two men sat with headsets on. A third man, taller, in a khaki shirt with the sleeves rolled, stood with his back to the door and was, at the moment Donald Gennaro entered, talking in a low quiet voice to a fourth man in a bush jacket who was holding what looked, at a glance, like a cattle prod. John Arnold did not smile when he said this. John Arnold was, Donald Gennaro saw, a man who had been awake for a very long time. title: Chapter 12: The Sick Stegosaur
text: It had been ninety minutes since the species count on the control-room screen had stopped at one thousand nine hundred and thirty-two when Ellie Sattler, in the lead Land Cruiser of the standard tour route, set her left palm flat against the inside of the door and felt, through the door, the slow rocking motion of a vehicle running, with no driver, on a buried induction track at twenty-five kilometers an hour through a tropical forest she had not, four hours earlier, believed could exist anywhere on the surface of the planet. Ellie Sattler’s right palm rested on the field journal in her lap. Ellie Sattler’s field journal had, on its top page, three small headings written in the small careful left-handed print: Soil , Canopy , Understory , with the notes for the first two left half-finished because, three minutes earlier, the tour-route narrator on the speaker had told them that they were now approaching the southern stegosaur range, and Ellie Sattler had stopped writing. Beside her in the front passenger seat, Alan Grant was leaning forward with his slouch hat off and his palm against the windshield, watching the road. The road came around a long left bend. The road came out into an open meadow. In the meadow, motionless, in a wide patch of crushed bunchgrass at the edge of a thicket of low pale-leaved bushes, stood a stegosaur. The stegosaur was twenty feet long. The stegosaur had the great vertical bone plates along its back that Ellie Sattler had drawn in her sketchbook, from the museum cast in Pittsburgh, when Ellie Sattler had been thirteen and had not yet understood that drawing the bones was a thing one was allowed to do for a living. The stegosaur was breathing slowly, in long wet labored breaths, and the great leathery plates along its spine drooped a fraction with each exhalation, as if the plates carried, at the base, just enough live tissue to register that the animal was unwell. title: Chapter 13: The Field with No Mothers
text: It had been twelve minutes since Gerry Harding’s jeep had pulled away across the meadow when Alan Grant, of the University of Denver, who had not got back into the lead Land Cruiser with the others, who had instead said give me five minutes and waited until Ellie Sattler had walked across the wet grass to the Land Cruiser without looking back at him in the small careful way Ellie Sattler did not look back at Alan Grant when Ellie Sattler understood what Alan Grant was about to do, knelt by himself in the bunchgrass at the foot of the small black volcanic outcrop and put both palms down into the pile of gizzard stones. Alan Grant’s right palm, in the cooling air, carried the small grit of basalt dust the stones rubbed off when the stones were moved against one another. Alan Grant’s left palm carried, between thumb and index finger, a small bitter green chinaberry that Alan Grant had picked off the lower bough of the nearest Melia shrub and had not, yet, decided what to do with. Alan Grant’s slouch hat lay upside-down beside him on the outcrop, and into the inside of the slouch hat Alan Grant had set, in the small careful way of a paleontologist working a surface site without a brush kit, three things in three places: a single worn gizzard stone at the band, a single chinaberry at the crown, and, in the small canvas notch sewn into the inner lining for a Sharpie, a folded bandanna. Alan Grant began to take the pile apart. He took it apart in the slow way Alan Grant took things apart at a dig, which was one stone at a time, with a half-second of looking before each stone went into the second pile, and a half-second of looking after each stone had gone, in case a thing that had been hidden by the moved stone was now visible. The stones were warm at the bottom of the pile. The stones smelled, faintly, of the sour acidic smell of an animal stomach that had passed through the air for a few weeks but had not, entirely, stopped being the inside of an animal stomach. title: Chapter 14: Two Hundred and Ninety-Two
text: It had been thirty minutes since Alan Grant had finished the count of the procompsognathid hatchlings on the slope above the maintenance road and had gotten back into the Land Cruiser, and in that thirty minutes the air over the central highland of Isla Nublar had thickened, the way air thickens before a storm, and the cicadas had stopped, and the radio in the dashboard of the Land Cruiser had begun to fill up with the small staticky sound a radio in Costa Rica fills up with in advance of weather coming in off the Pacific. In the control room at the visitor center, Ray Arnold lit another cigarette. Ray Arnold had, by the count taped to the inside of the cabinet door behind his console, smoked nineteen cigarettes since seven that morning. Ray Arnold was the chief engineer of Jurassic Park. Ray Arnold had been chief engineer of two military tracking installations and one offshore oil platform before John Hammond had hired him on a four-year contract to run a piece of software written by a man named Dennis Nedry who, Ray Arnold often said to no one in particular, was the worst programmer Ray Arnold had ever been required to live with. Ray Arnold looked at the central monitor. The central monitor was the count board. The count board read, in green serif numerals at the top of the screen, Total Animals 238 . Below the green numerals there was a table of fifteen rows, one row per species, each row showing an Expected number and a Found number, and beside both numbers a third number, which Ray Arnold had explained to Donald Gennaro that morning was the version. Tyrannosaurs 2 and 2, version 4.1. Velociraptors 8 and 8, version 3.0. Procompsognathids 49 and 49, version 3.9. The expected and the found matched, in every row, for every species, and the total at the bottom of the table was 238, and the total had been 238 every time Ray Arnold had looked at the count board for nine months. title: Chapter 15: The Wrong Turn
text: Dennis Nedry’s hands were on the keyboard at six twenty-two in the evening. The keyboard was the operator keyboard at the second console of the control room, the small one in the corner Ray Arnold did not like to use because the swivel chair under it was broken, and the small swivel chair was, in the small careful interior accounting Dennis Nedry had been running on the geometry of the control room since arriving on the island, the chair Dennis Nedry preferred. From the small chair Dennis Nedry could see the wall clock and the door to the hallway and the back of Ray Arnold’s head, in that order, without turning his neck. Dennis Nedry had practiced not turning his neck. Dennis Nedry, by the small interior accounting, had three minutes. He typed system.security.set off . He typed fence.east.power off . He typed fence.maint.access off . He typed display.console retain normal . He typed whte_rabt.obj and pressed return. The screen flickered. The green serif numerals at the top of the count board did the small careful thing of staying exactly where they were. text: It had been twelve minutes since the two Toyota Land Cruisers, running on the buried induction cable that fed power to their drive wheels along the central scenic loop of Jurassic Park, had stopped on the rise of the hill above the tyrannosaur paddock. title: Chapter 18: The Rex Breaks the Wire
text: It had been forty-one minutes since the two Toyota Land Cruisers had stopped on the road north of the goat field with no white painted line under their bumpers and no recorded voice on the speakers and no reply on the dashboard radio. The rain came down on the roofs of the cars in the slow heavy way the rain comes down on a tin roof in a Costa Rican lowland in the flat hour after a tropical storm has come ashore. Tim Murphy, in the front seat of the second car with the night-vision goggles tipped up onto his forehead, watched the foliage to his left for movement. The foliage moved by the small careful amount the foliage had been moving in the wind for the past forty minutes, and then the foliage moved by a different amount, and Tim Murphy lowered the goggles. In the goggles, the trees behind the chain-link cyclone fence were a flat phosphor-green wall. title: Chapter 19: The Boy in the Tree
text: Tim Murphy held the branch with both hands and did not let go. The branch was the second branch up from the place where the Land Cruiser had stopped, which was no longer a place because the Land Cruiser had been picked up by the Tyrannosaurus rex and lifted six meters into the air and dropped, and the dropping had not been onto the road, which would have been the road, and would have made sense, but onto the lower fork of the kapok tree at the edge of the road, where the Land Cruiser had wedged like a child’s tin toy a careless adult had tossed by accident into a high shelf. Tim Murphy had crawled out the broken passenger window. Tim Murphy had crawled up. Tim Murphy was now nine meters above the road. The Land Cruiser was three meters below him, hanging in the fork by its rear axle, and the rear axle, by the small careful audit Tim Murphy was running on it from above, was bending. He wedged his foot. He wedged his foot the way his grandfather had told him to wedge a foot in the crotch of an apple tree at the Hammond house in Sussex when Tim Murphy had been seven. Heel down. Toe pointing in. Body weight onto the heel, never onto the toe, because the toe slips and the heel does not. Tim Murphy had not known, at seven, why his grandfather, who was John Hammond, who lived in a great house in Sussex with a garden and a butler and three small electric trams that ran in the grounds, had wanted to teach a small American grandson the small careful trick of wedging a foot in a kapok-shaped fork. title: Chapter 20: The River and the Swimmer
text: The raft pushed out from the rock at four-eleven in the morning when the river current took the rubber bow and turned it sideways and Alan Grant, who had pushed off with one foot in the shallow water and the other knee on the deck, fell forward and caught himself on his palms. Lex Murphy did not laugh. Tim Murphy, who was awake again and was wearing one of Alan Grant’s spare T-shirts that came down to his knees, did not laugh either. Tim Murphy was holding the bow line with both hands. The bow line was wet. title: Chapter 21: The Aviary Lodge
text: Lex Murphy stepped onto the wet planks of the aviary dock with the baseball glove tucked under her left arm and her right hand in the back pocket of Alan Grant’s khaki field shirt. title: Chapter 22: Dawn at Building Four
text: It had been six hours since the raft had pushed off from the rock above the river when the first sun came up over the ridge behind the Pteratops Lodge and put a long flat orange light through the smashed picture window of the lodge dining room and onto the back of a small gray-brown animal the size of a Labrador puppy that was, at the moment the light reached it, asleep on the carpet under the dining table. Tim Murphy saw it first. Tim Murphy had been the one not asleep. Alan Grant, on the carpet by the bolted door, had finally let his head go back against the wall at five-thirty in the morning, with one arm still around Lex Murphy’s shoulder and Lex Murphy’s head against his chest, and Tim Murphy, who had been watching them from the couch by the cold fireplace, had decided that the small careful thing to do was to keep watch. Tim Murphy was good at keeping watch. Tim Murphy had kept watch on the back seat of his father’s Volvo on three separate weekends in 1988, when his father had gone into the apartment of a woman the family did not know and had not come out for an hour and a half, and had told Tim Murphy that the man’s job, when the man was the older man in the car, was to keep watch. Tim Murphy had not slept. The animal under the table had. He crossed the carpet on his hands and knees. The animal under the table was a baby. The three horns on the head were nubs, two above the eyes and one above the nose, the size of a man’s thumb. The bony frill was soft and pink-veined at the edges. The animal had its eyes closed. title: Chapter 23: The Maintenance Shed
text: It had been five hours since the auxiliary generator had begun running, and John Arnold, of Cambridge, Massachusetts, who held a doctorate in electrical engineering and had been the chief engineer of Jurassic Park for eleven months, had not, in those five hours, thought to look at the small green readout on the lower right corner of his console which would have told him that the main grid was off. The printout was on the desk between them now. Wu held it out. Muldoon held the radio. Hammond stood at the door with his cane and said nothing. The line that mattered, Wu told them, was the line at the third quarter of the page. The line that mattered said Warning: Fence Status and the bracketed code beside it, and the column beside that, which read Operative, Aux Power. John Arnold looked at the line. John Arnold had taught a graduate seminar on system integration at MIT for two semesters, before he had taken the InGen contract, and John Arnold knew, looking at the line, exactly what the line meant. Aux power. Aux power did not produce enough amperage to charge the perimeter fences. The fences had been dark for five hours. The velociraptor compound, which sat behind a perimeter fence, had been, for five hours, a paddock with a wire-mesh wall around it that ran no current. “Christ,” John Arnold said. From somewhere off through the trees, on the far side of the visitor center, a Tican workman screamed. Muldoon began to talk fast. He went around the room handing out the portable radios. He gave one to Wu. He gave one to Hammond. He gave one to Gennaro, who took it with both hands and dropped it once and picked it up again. Muldoon told Wu to stay in the control room, because Wu was the only other man on the island who could work the touchscreens. He told Hammond to go back to the lodge. He told Hammond to lock the gates and stay behind them and not argue. Hammond opened his mouth, then closed it, then opened it again. “What are you going to do to my animals?” Hammond said.

title: Chapter 24: Bait at the Lodge Fence
text: It had been four minutes since the shoulder launcher had gone off behind the visitor center when Ellie Sattler, of Berkeley, California, who had walked across cattle pasture in three countries and had once put her hand into a fer-de-lance den on a bet, stood inside the front door of the Safari Lodge and laced her running shoes. She tied the left one twice. She always tied the left one twice. It was a thing she had done at twelve, when she had run track at her father’s high school in Wisconsin, and it was a thing she did still, when she was about to go out into a piece of ground she did not, for whatever reason, trust. Henry Wu was at the radio. Henry Wu was looking at the stairwell to the roof. Henry Wu was, in the small careful internal way Wu kept his face from showing what Wu was feeling, terrified. Robert Muldoon was sitting on the floor against the wall by the sideboard with his right boot off and his ankle wrapped in a tea towel that was already wet through. Muldoon had come down off the embankment behind the visitor center with the swelling already in him. Muldoon had got over the lodge fence by hand and elbow because he had not been able to put weight on the foot, and Wu and Harding had got him through the gate and dropped him here on the polished wood floor, and Muldoon had not yet stood up. Hammond was in the small kitchen. Hammond was making, of all things, a pot of coffee, on a burner that ran off the propane line, because the electric hob would not light. Ellie Sattler heard the small careful clink of a teaspoon on the rim of a porcelain cup, and Ellie Sattler did not, in that moment, know whether to laugh or to walk into the kitchen and take the cup out of Hammond’s hand and pour it on the floor. She did neither. She finished the second knot. Above them, on the slanted glass pyramid that was the skylight over Malcolm’s room, two velociraptors were chewing on the second of the three steel bars set into the frame. title: Chapter 25: The Touchscreen Child
text: Tim Murphy was eleven years old. Tim Murphy had at home in Chappaqua, New York, a Commodore 64 with a flickering composite monitor, an Apple IIe in his school’s library that he was allowed on for forty minutes each Friday, a paperback copy of Hackers by Steven Levy that he had read three times, and a clipping from a 1987 Byte magazine that showed an artist’s rendering of an infrared touchscreen interface for naval combat systems, which Tim Murphy had folded in half and kept inside the back cover of the Levy book. Tim Murphy had never used a touchscreen. Tim Murphy stood in front of one now. The control room at the Jurassic Park visitor center was lit only by the four monitors at the center console, which were on, which meant that some power was on, which meant that John Arnold had reached the maintenance shed and had done the thing he had gone there to do, which was, Tim understood, the only useful thing anyone in the lodge had been able to think of. The monitors showed colored rectangles. The colored rectangles were buttons. text: The supply boat Anne B was a forty-six-foot wooden trawler out of Puntarenas, owned by the cooperative Pesquería del Pacífico Sur and contracted by InGen for the Sunday and Wednesday turnaround. title: Chapter 27: Down to the Waterworks
text: It had been forty minutes since Alan Grant had asked Donald Gennaro, in the small bare office at the back of the maintenance shed, whether Donald Gennaro would come down with him into the waterworks. The handheld was a slim metal slab the size of a paperback, the kind of unit the InGen survey teams had been issued the previous summer, with a small green liquid-crystal display and a soft rubber thumb-pad and a row of four hard keys along the bottom. text: It had been twenty minutes since John Hammond had walked out of the safari lodge by the back kitchen door without telling anyone he was going. John Hammond had told himself, in the kitchen, that he was going for a short walk to the small lookout above the path where, on the planning model in his office in San Francisco the previous spring, he had imagined the visitor cottages going. John Hammond had told himself, on the kitchen step, that the walk would do him good. John Hammond had told himself, at the head of the path, that the path was the same path he had walked twice before with the surveyor in the dry season, and that the path was easy. John Hammond was, on all three counts, lying to himself in the small careful way old men lie to themselves when they have decided to be alone for a few minutes and have not, yet, said the word alone . The path was not the same path. The path, in the eight months since the last grading, had been narrowed by the rain. The packed dirt of the surveyor’s afternoon had been cut, in two places, by small drainage scars where the storm of the previous night had run off the upper slope. The lower section, where the path bent past a stand of tree ferns and dropped to the small culverted stream below, was, by the look of it from the upper switchback, a meter narrower than John Hammond remembered. John Hammond had walked the path twice, with the surveyor. The surveyor had been, both times, holding John Hammond’s elbow. John Hammond, who was seventy-six, who had walked unassisted from the kitchen door, who had refused at the door to take with him the radio handset Ed Regis had left in the kitchen for the purpose of being taken on exactly this kind of walk, did not, at the head of the upper switchback, turn around. He stepped down. He stepped down a second time. On the third step the path moved. The path did not move in the way a thing moves when it is grasped and pushed.
