title: Chapter 30: The Beach Migration
text: It had been an hour since Alan Grant had climbed out of the service hatch of the storm drain trunk junction and had walked, alone, down the safari road in the direction of the eastern beach. Alan Grant had not, on the way down to the beach, told Donald Gennaro where he was going. Donald Gennaro had been, when Alan Grant had left the maintenance shed, sitting on a folding chair with both hands flat on the metal table and not, by any measure Alan Grant had been able to do at the door, ready to be told. Alan Grant carried the handheld in his hand. The handheld still read, on the green display, thirty-four. Alan Grant had not, since climbing up out of the corridor, pressed any of the keys. The number did not need to be added to. The number was, by itself, the number. Alan Grant came out of the jungle at the back of the beach. The beach was the eastern beach. The beach was the long pale crescent the printed park map called Strand North-East, which the construction crews had cleared the previous spring of driftwood and which the storm of two nights ago had, by the small thoroughness of the storm, restocked with a long line of black driftwood and tangled palm-frond at the high-tide mark. The high-tide mark ran the length of the beach in a slow shallow scallop along the wet line where the surf, at this hour, no longer reached. In the wet sand below the high-tide mark there were tracks. The tracks were three-toed. The tracks were, by the small measurements Alan Grant did with the heel of his hand at the first set, perhaps twenty centimeters from claw-tip to heel-pad, with the central toe longer than the outer two and the small dimple of the killing claw drawn back clear of the print, which was the print of an animal walking and not of an animal striking. The tracks were the tracks of velociraptors. Alan Grant crouched at the first set. The first set was a single line. title: Chapter 31: The Straight Line Into the Mountains
text: It had been six days since the helicopters from the Costa Rican Air Force had come in low over the eastern beach of Isla Nublar at first light, taken the eleven survivors off the strand at the base of the lodge path, and lifted away to the southeast under a cloud cover that was, by the time the helicopters reached the mainland, beginning to break. The hotel was the Hotel Cariari. The hotel was not the hotel the Costa Rican government had originally placed the eleven survivors in. The first hotel, on the night of the helicopter lift, had been a small four-story building near the airport in San José where the Costa Rican Ministry of Health had a standing arrangement with the night manager for the boarding of foreign nationals who were, in the careful phrase the woman from the Ministry had used at the door, under medical observation. On the second morning the eleven had been moved to the Cariari, which had a walled garden, and a security gate, and a phone exchange that did not, by the small careful test Donald Gennaro had run on the second afternoon, place outgoing calls. Alan Grant was in room 304. Ellie Sattler was in room 306. Donald Gennaro was in room 312, with the wall side facing the staff corridor. Tim Murphy and Lex Murphy were, by the explicit instruction of the woman from the Ministry, in a single double room on the second floor with a paid Costa Rican social worker who slept on a cot at the foot of the children’s bed. The grandfather, who had been listed on the manifest from the helicopter as deceased, had not, by the time the helicopters lifted off the beach, been the only listing on the manifest the Costa Rican military physician at the makeshift triage on the helipad had marked with an X. Robert Muldoon was in room 220. Henry Wu, Ray Arnold, Dennis Nedry, John Hammond, and Ed Regis had not been brought off the island. The island had been firebombed on the third morning. title: Chapter 1: The Beach That Should Have Been Empty
text: It had been three hours since Richard Levine stopped believing in coincidence. The helicopter flew low enough that the sand seemed to slide under its skids. On one side was the Pacific, flat and bruised by the late light. On the other side was jungle so dense it did not look like trees, but a single green wall that had learned how to breathe. There were no roads. There were no houses. There were not even the usual bright scraps of plastic and fishing line that marked a beach people had touched. That was the first wrong thing. Richard Levine sat beside the pilot with his long knees folded too high and his binoculars hanging against his chest. He had spent thirty years making himself intolerable to fools, students, collectors, museum donors, and any colleague who used the word “lizard” around a dinosaur. He did not mind being disliked. Dislike was cheap. Accuracy was expensive. Marty Guitierrez, behind him, had already stopped trying to make conversation. Marty Guitierrez had known Richard Levine for seventeen years. He knew the posture. The tucked chin. The fingers locked under the jaw. The entire body held still so that the eyes could do all the moving. It meant Richard Levine had seen a pattern and was waiting for the world to confess. “There,” the pilot said. The helicopter banked. At first Richard Levine saw only driftwood. Then the driftwood had ribs. The shape lay high on the beach where the tide had thrown it and then retreated, as if the sea itself had decided it wanted no further responsibility. Flies lifted in a black cloud. The body was swollen, half-buried, green-black in the places where decay had opened it and pale in the places where the skin had stretched tight. From the air it looked like something badly made. Too long here, too heavy there. Neck wrong. Pelvis wrong. Tail wrong. Richard Levine leaned forward until the safety strap bit into his shoulder. “Put us down.” The pilot said something in Spanish. Marty Guitierrez answered him, patient and une

title: Chapter 2: Levine's Last Call
text: It had been six hours since Richard Levine put the tissue sample in his bag. At the San José airport, under fluorescent lights that made every traveler look ill, Richard Levine stood before a row of international telephones and considered how much truth could safely fit into one recorded message. Not much, he decided. Truth had weight. Weight attracted attention. Attention had already arrived on the beach with fuel cans. Richard Levine shifted the receiver to his other hand. His backpack rested against his shoe. The field pouch lay open on the narrow metal shelf below the phone, just enough for him to see the small sealed sleeve inside. A wet green-gray scrap. A useless thing to anyone passing by. A beginning, if it reached the correct hands. The recorded voice on the line asked him to leave a message. Richard Levine closed his eyes. “Ian,” he said, “this is Levine. The Costa Rica trip produced a specimen. Bad condition, but enough. Southern coast. Grid reference BB-seventeen on the map. Repeat, BB-seventeen. The animal is not on the expected list.” He stopped. A woman with a red suitcase moved past him. A boy dropped a paper cup. The loudspeaker announced a flight in Spanish, then English, then a version of English that made the words soft at the edges. Richard Levine looked at the glass beyond the phones. No one was watching him. That was exactly what a watched man always thought first. “There is also a wound,” he said into the receiver. “A deep wound along the lateral femur. I do not like what it implies. I am sending tissue separately. Have it examined immediately. Do not wait for me.” He almost hung up. Then he added, “And Ian… if you decide to be clever, be clever quickly.” He replaced the receiver. For a moment Richard Levine remained with his hand on the phone, listening to the dead plastic silence of it. Malcolm would mock him. title: Chapter 3: The Equation That Would Not Behave
text: It had been two days since Ian Malcolm received the cylinder. For most of those two days, Ian Malcolm did what human beings did best. He avoided the fact in front of him and called it judgment. The cylinder sat on his desk. Stainless steel. Medical grade. Over-labeled by a man who trusted labels more than people. A biohazard symbol on the lid. A shipping receipt tucked under the strap. Richard Levine’s blocky handwriting on the side. Ian Malcolm disliked Richard Levine’s handwriting. He disliked Richard Levine’s certainty. He disliked, most of all, that Richard Levine was often wrong in small ways and then suddenly right in one large way that made everyone else’s life worse. Malcolm stood in the office without his cane for eleven seconds. He counted them because pain made a man either sentimental or mathematical, and Malcolm had chosen mathematical years ago. At twelve seconds, his injured leg began to insist on history. At fifteen, he sat. Beverly, his assistant, stood near the door. “Do you want me to call the lab again?” “No.” “They said they could rush it.” “Rushing is what people do when they have already decided the answer.” “And you haven’t?” Malcolm looked at the cylinder. “I have decided several answers. I dislike all of them.” He broke the seal. Cold vapor unrolled over his fingers. Inside the cylinder was a small bag, a folded note, and a tissue fragment the color of old seaweed. Malcolm did not touch the tissue. He knew exactly how much theater there was in not touching it. He also knew that fear was one of the few human emotions with a proper respect for evidence. Beverly leaned closer. “Is that from Costa Rica?” Malcolm looked up. Too quickly. Beverly noticed. Beverly noticed everything after payroll and before panic. That was why she was valuable. “Send it to Elizabeth Gelman,” Malcolm said. “Directly. Not through the department. Not through receiving. Hand carry it.” “What should I tell her?” “Tell her I am being dramatic.” “She knows that.” “Then te

title: Chapter 4: A Map With No Tour Roads
text: It had been twelve minutes since the island appeared on the monitor. No one in Levine’s apartment spoke first. That was how Kelly Curtis knew the map was worse than a map. Adults loved maps. Adults pointed at maps and made plans and told children to stand back. This map made the adults stand still. The screen showed roads, utility corridors, worker housing, a laboratory, a power plant, holding pens, docks, gas lines, thermal lines, and one clean label sitting at the center of the old file structure like a nail through paper. SITE B. Ian Malcolm lowered himself into a chair. He did not look surprised. That was the frightening part. Surprise would have meant the world had broken cleanly. Malcolm looked instead like a man watching a bridge fail exactly where he had once marked the rust. “It’s not a park,” Arby said. “No,” Malcolm said. “There are no tour roads,” Kelly said. “No.” “Then what is it?” Malcolm’s mouth tightened. Jack Thorne answered for him. “A production facility.” Kelly looked back at the map. Production made her think of factories and lunch meat and cars moving down assembly lines. The island did not look like a place where a company made animals. It looked like a place where a company had hidden the part of the trick that would make customers leave the theater. Thorne started moving. That was different from thinking. Thinking made people slow. Thorne’s thinking came out as hands. He called Eddie Carr. He called the air freight broker. He called a man named Jenkins and began using phrases Kelly had only heard in movies: export papers, temporary equipment permit, landing weight, cargo manifest. He opened a cabinet in Levine’s office and found satellite printouts. He swept them into a tube without asking permission. “We leave tonight,” Thorne said. “You can’t,” Kelly said. “I can.” “You don’t even know if Dr. Levine is alive.” Thorne looked at her. “That is why we leave tonight.” Malcolm was still seated. title: Chapter 5: Thorne Builds the Impossible
text: It had been four hours since Jack Thorne stopped pretending the machines were ready. They looked ready. That was the insult. The trailers gleamed under the shop lights, enormous and clean and sealed, their skins reinforced until they were closer to field bunkers than vehicles. The Explorer sat beside them with its engine bay open and its solar panels bolted down like black scales. Equipment cases stood in rows. Fuel, batteries, radio relays, medical packs, tools, winches, cables, climbing rope, field notebooks, portable scopes, spare tires, water, food, more water. Everything had a label. Everything had a weakness. Jack Thorne moved from one weakness to the next. “That bracket will shear if the trailer twists.” Eddie Carr leaned under the chassis with a flashlight in his teeth. “It won’t shear.” “It will shear if five tons hits the rear corner.” Eddie pulled the flashlight from his mouth and stared at him. “Five tons of what?” Thorne did not answer. Eddie waited. Thorne said, “Add the plate.” Eddie added the plate. That was their language. Thorne liked Eddie Carr because Eddie argued only until the metal proved the argument. Eddie was young, compact, anxious, and better with machines than he knew. His hands understood stress before his mouth found the sentence. Tonight those hands kept checking the same systems again and again. Solar seals. Battery mounts. Radio couplings. Door latches. The door latches most of all. Malcolm watched from the bay entrance. “You know,” Malcolm said, “there is a point where preparation becomes decorative.” “There is a point where talking becomes noise,” Thorne said. “I crossed it years ago.” “Proud of you.” Malcolm came closer, leaning on the cane only when he thought no one was looking. Thorne saw him do it. Thorne said nothing. Friendship had rules. One of them was that a man could keep the performance that got him through the hour. “You still haven’t told Eddie enough,” Malcolm said. “I told Eddie the vehicles have to withstand large

title: Chapter 6: Two Children in the Cargo Hold
text: It had been nine minutes since the helicopters lifted away. Eddie Carr watched them shrink above the crater rim and tried not to hate the sound of departure. Machines should come back when a man needed them. Helicopters did not care. They became dots. Then insects. Then nothing. The island remained. Heat pressed down on the clearing. Jungle rose around them in layered green. The trailers sat where the cargo crew had dropped them. The Explorer waited beside a stack of sealed cases. Beyond the clearing, the old road vanished under vines. Jack Thorne checked the radio. Ian Malcolm watched the trees. Eddie watched the sky. “Tomorrow morning,” Thorne said. “What?” “The helicopters come back tomorrow morning.” Eddie nodded. “Right.” He did not feel better. Tomorrow morning belonged to men who believed clocks had authority in jungles. They assembled quickly. Thorne wanted the convoy moving before the heat climbed. Malcolm wanted to find Levine before the island produced an argument no one could win. Eddie wanted thirty more minutes with the vehicles and knew he would not get it. He checked the solar readouts. He checked battery charge. He checked the dart rifles because Thorne told him to and because the labels on the venom cartridges made his hands sweat. “This stuff kills?” “Instantly,” Thorne said. “That’s not comforting.” “It isn’t supposed to comfort the animal.” Malcolm looked at the gun. “Human civilization, summarized in one sentence.” The convoy entered the jungle. The road had once been paved. Now it was a memory under roots, mud, and leaf fall. The Explorer led. Thorne drove the trailer. Malcolm sat beside him with the computer on his knees and the expression of a man reading bad news from an instrument panel. They found the first animals at the stream. Small green bodies moved at the edge of the water. One, then three, then a dozen. They stepped with quick birdlike jerks, tails held straight, heads cocking. Their eyes were bright and unafraid. title: Chapter 7: High Hide
text: It had been one hour since the valley proved Richard Levine had not been insane. That did not make Richard Levine safe. The tag signal blinked on the screen and pulled them deeper into the island. It moved, paused, moved again. Sometimes the signal vanished behind terrain. Sometimes it returned ahead of them where no road should have allowed it. Thorne drove faster than the trailer liked. Eddie cursed every branch that struck the solar panels. Malcolm sat very still. From the trailer, Arby guided them. He had confessed by accident. Technically Kelly had confessed first, by turning on the radio and saying, “Dr. Thorne, don’t be mad.” Thorne had been mad. Then the tag signal shifted, and anger became a luxury. Now Kelly and Arby were locked in the trailer by order, which was not much different from being useful by opportunity. “Signal bearing east,” Arby said. “Past the main facility.” “Any roads?” “Old service roads. Maybe.” “Maybe is not a road,” Eddie said. Kelly, watching the camera feeds beside Arby, saw the valley ahead and forgot the argument. The high hide stood folded on its trailer mount like a metal insect. Thorne had wanted to leave it. Levine, over radio when they finally raised him, wanted it deployed immediately. Levine’s voice came thin through static, alive and exasperating. “If you brought the hide, use the hide.” Malcolm closed his eyes. “He is alive.” “You sound disappointed,” Thorne said. “I am preparing myself.” They assembled the hide because Richard Levine insisted on research even while being rescued, and because the valley below was too large to understand from the road. The structure rose in sections. Aluminum legs locked. Cross-braces snapped into place. A small platform climbed above the grass. From there, the herds became a moving diagram. Duckbills near water. title: Chapter 8: Sarah Harding Lands on Isla Sorna
text: It had been twenty-seven hours since Sarah Harding received Malcolm’s message. She had listened to it once in Nairobi. She had listened to it again in the airport. She had listened to it a third time in Costa Rica, standing under a leaking awning while rain came down hard enough to make the street foam. Ian sounded calm in the recording. That was how Sarah knew he was afraid. Sarah Harding did not collect afraid men. She did not enjoy rescuing them. She had built a life around animals because animals had the courtesy to show their intentions in posture, scent, distance, and teeth. Men preferred language. It slowed everything down. The official at the harbor had already told her no. The boat captain had told her no. The weather had told her no. Sarah Harding had heard no in universities, in field camps, from grant committees, from men who believed a woman studying predators was a charming contradiction. No was not information. No was a door sound. She found another door. It belonged to a fishing boat that smelled of diesel, bait, and old wood. Three men were loading equipment. One was a heavy man with academic hands and an appetite he did not hide. One was nervous, watching the water as if it might sue him. The third looked at Sarah with a stillness that made her dislike him before he spoke. “Dr. Harding?” he said. She stopped. “Do I know you?” “Lewis Dodgson.” That name reached her through memory: Ian, irritated; Richard Levine, contemptuous; some old scandal involving biotech, ethics, and the kind of man who thought both words were negotiable. Sarah adjusted the strap on her bag. “You’re going to the island.” Dodgson smiled. “It appears we all are.” The boat left in bad weather. Sarah stood near the rail and watched the cliffs grow out of the sea. Isla Sorna did not look like a secret laboratory. It looked older than secrets. title: Chapter 9: The First Herd
text: It had been forty minutes since Richard Levine returned to civilization and began abusing it. “That hinge is in the wrong place.” Eddie Carr stared at him. “Nice to see you too.” “The hide sways.” “It’s fifteen feet tall.” “It sways too much.” “So would I if you climbed me.” Richard Levine ignored this because Richard Levine ignored most useful social feedback. He had found the binoculars, a notebook, and three energy bars. Now, restored to the minimum operating condition of his species, he stood on the high hide platform and looked down at the valley like a man who had been personally promised it. Kelly climbed up beside him. “What are those?” “Parasaurolophus. Likely. The crest is unmistakable.” “And those?” “Hypsilophodonts, possibly. Don’t lean on that rail.” “You just leaned on it.” “I understand my center of gravity.” Kelly leaned anyway. The valley was not peaceful. That was the first correction her eyes made. From far away, the animals looked slow and gentle, an enormous mural of prehistoric life. From the hide, with the field glasses to her eyes, she saw motion everywhere. Heads lifting. Tails shifting. Young animals pressed near larger bodies. Herds adjusting distance around water. One group giving way before another without any animal appearing to command it. It was not a picture. It was an argument being held by bodies. Malcolm came up slowly, unhappy with the ladder and the entire concept of height. “You are all very pleased,” he said. Levine did not lower the binoculars. “Yes.” “That is usually the stage immediately before casualties.” “Ian, must you poison every extraordinary moment?” “No. Reality often gets there first.” Arby, below at the console, called through the radio. “The acoustic monitor is picking up low frequency calls. Repeating pattern.” Levine brightened. “Excellent.” Malcolm did not. “Repeating how?” “Groups answer each other. Like signals.” Kelly watched the Parasaurolophus lift its head. title: Chapter 10: The Village in the Jungle
text: It had been eleven minutes since the Velociraptor looked up. The expedition left the high hide standing in the valley like a bad decision. No one argued. That was how Eddie knew the fear was serious. Richard Levine always argued unless the world had briefly become too large for him. Kelly and Arby were ordered into the trailer. This time they went. Malcolm sat with one hand pressed against his leg and watched the grass through the rear camera. The Velociraptors did not follow onto the open road. That should have been comforting. It was not. The road climbed toward the main complex, then bent through a stand of wet trees and revealed the village. Small cottages stood in rows beneath vines. A larger house rose at the end, its balconies sagging, windows dark. There had once been lawns. Now ferns grew waist high. There had once been a path. Now roots crossed it like cables. On one wall, half-covered by leaves, an InGen safety sign still insisted that the future was made here. Thorne stopped the convoy. Eddie said, “Maybe we should keep moving.” “We need records,” Malcolm said. “We need to not be eaten.” “Both goals have merit.” They went in because the tag map showed the village connected to the laboratory network, and because Richard Levine believed abandoned offices were less dangerous than unread documents. This was not a majority opinion. The first building smelled of mold, dust, animal droppings, and time. Kelly stayed close to Arby. She did not like the offices. The rooms were ordinary in a way that made them worse. Desks. Chairs. Coffee mugs. A calendar still pinned to a wall. Someone had once sat here complaining about lunch while animals that should not exist moved somewhere beyond the fence. Arby found an old terminal. “No power.” “There is geothermal power,” Eddie said. “Plant’s still warm. Some circuits still active.” Malcolm looked at him. “How do you know?” “Because the junction boxes outside are showing red standby lights.” Thorne smiled faintly. “That’s

title: Chapter 11: Workers' Housing
text: It had been four seconds since Arby said under us. Four seconds was too long. Jack Thorne reached the trailer first and did not open the door. That restraint saved them. His hand went to the latch, stopped, and moved instead to the side camera panel. The external feed showed the underside: axles, shadow, dangling cables, mud. And a small green head looking directly into the lens. Procompsognathus. Not one. Many. Eddie arrived with the rifle, breathing hard. “Compys?” “Compys,” Thorne said. “I thought they were harmless.” Malcolm reached them and leaned against the trailer, not from ease. “That is a sentence human beings say shortly before amending the definition of harmless.” Inside, Kelly and Arby had climbed onto the counter. The little animals had come through a service gap under the rear storage bay, attracted by food wrappers, warmth, and the universal animal belief that any new opening in the world should be investigated with teeth. They chittered across the floor. Kelly held a metal stool like a weapon. Arby stood behind her with a fire extinguisher, eyes wide, hands steady. “Are they poisonous?” Kelly shouted. “Possibly,” Malcolm shouted back. “That is not helpful!” Sarah Harding’s voice came from outside. “Move away from the counter.” Everyone turned. Sarah Harding stood at the edge of the village, wet, cut, filthy, and alive. For one full second, Malcolm simply stared at her. Then Sarah said, “Ian, if you’re finished looking guilty, open the roof vent.” Sarah solved the compy problem in three moves. First, vent open. Second, food out. Third, people still. The Procompsognathus followed the thrown food with more enthusiasm than malice, pouring out from under the trailer in a green, squeaking stream. Eddie hated every second of not shooting them. Thorne hated every second of having built a trailer with a service gap large enough for history’s smallest revenge. Kelly did not climb down until the last tail vanished. title: Chapter 12: The Nursery Floor
text: It had been eighteen minutes since the juvenile Velociraptor looked into the camera. Richard Levine wanted to go to the nursery. No one was surprised. Surprise required the possibility that Richard Levine might behave sensibly. That possibility had died somewhere between the burning beach and the tyrannosaur nest. “We need to understand breeding distribution,” Levine said. Sarah Harding was checking the darts. “No.” “The nursery may contain records.” “No.” “Eggshells, growth data, nesting material—” “Richard.” “What?” “No.” Levine turned to Malcolm. “Ian, tell her.” Malcolm looked up from the old InGen memos. “I find her argument elegant.” “It is not an argument.” “That is its elegance.” But the nursery did not leave them alone. The old network kept offering pieces: a live camera in one corridor, a door status, a temperature reading where no air-conditioning should still run. Arby found a maintenance map that showed the nursery connected to the lab and, beneath it, to a utility passage leading toward the worker village. That changed the room. Thorne leaned over the map. “A tunnel?” “Service crawl,” Arby said. “Maybe big enough.” “For people?” “For Kelly.” Kelly said, “Hey.” “For adults if they hate themselves.” Eddie said, “That sounds like our whole plan.” They needed the tunnel because the village was not defensible. Sarah said it first. The cottages were wood and rot. The storehouse had barred windows but weak walls. The trailers were stronger, but exposed. The lab had power and interior corridors. The tunnel, if passable, could become an escape route or a trap, depending on which end of the sentence the island preferred. So they went. Not all of them. Sarah, Thorne, Eddie, and Levine crossed to the nursery wing while Malcolm stayed with the kids in the trailer command station, which fooled no one into thinking the children were safe. title: Chapter 13: Raptors at the Fence
text: It had been one heartbeat since the jungle clicked. Then the fence moved. Not the whole fence. One patch of leaves beside it. A pattern separated from a pattern. Kelly Curtis had been staring directly at that place and still had not seen the animal until it chose to be seen. The adult Velociraptor stood behind the fence with its head angled sideways. It was not movie-large in the childish way Kelly had imagined monsters. It was worse. It was the correct size to enter human spaces. To pass through doors. To look into windows. To make every familiar measurement suddenly unsafe. The fence should have helped. It did not. The mesh was rusted in three places. The bottom edge had been lifted by roots and then bent by something stronger than roots. A person looking at it would see a barrier. The animal looking through it seemed to see an inconvenience, and that difference made Kelly’s mouth go dry. Sarah Harding lifted one hand, palm down. “No one run.” No one did. Another Velociraptor appeared to the left. Then a third. Their skins were striped and broken with greens and browns, the colors shifting under leaves until the eye refused certainty. They were not surrounding the group. Not yet. They were testing distance. Richard Levine whispered, “Chromatic adaptation…” Sarah did not look away from the animals. “Richard.” “Yes?” “If you finish that sentence, I will feed you to science.” Levine closed his mouth. Kelly had never seen an adult obey that fast. It made Sarah more frightening than the rifle. The first Velociraptor stepped closer. Eddie’s rifle moved half an inch. The animal’s eye snapped to the movement. “Eddie,” Sarah said. “I see it.” “It sees you seeing it.” “That’s not better.” Malcolm’s breathing had gone shallow. He stood near the trailer steps, one hand on the rail, face white but not with panic. Recognition was uglier than panic. “They remember structures,” he said. “What?” “Fences. Doors. Corners. Human shapes. These aren’t wild animals in the simple sense.

title: Chapter 14: Eggs Under Broken Glass
text: It had been thirty seconds since the trailer stopped obeying gravity. For Kelly, the world became angles. The floor was a wall. The ceiling was a slope. The table hung above her with instruments dangling from straps. Broken glass glittered where no glass should have been. Somewhere below, metal screamed with the slow violence of a ship tearing open. Ian Malcolm lay against the lower cabinets, breathing through his teeth. Sarah Harding was already moving. Kelly understood then that moving first was not courage. It was training. Sarah did not wait for fear to finish. She stepped over it. “Arby!” “Here!” Arby was wedged near the communications rack, face bleeding from a cut over one eye. Kelly crawled toward him. The trailer lurched again. Something huge struck the side, and the whole room swung closer to open air. Richard Levine clutched a support rail. “The eggs,” he said. Sarah stared at him. “What?” “The blood was from the nesting material. I had samples. I may have brushed—” “Richard,” Malcolm said from the floor, voice thin with pain, “if we survive, remind me to murder you academically.” The trailer shifted. The rear window broke out into jungle air. Beyond it, impossibly close, was the head of a Tyrannosaurus rex. Thorne came over the radio from outside. “Hold on! I’m on the connector!” “Connector?” Kelly shouted. Sarah looked toward the front of the trailer, now hanging lower over the drop. “The trailers are linked.” “Are we going over?” Sarah did not answer. That was the answer. She tied rope around Malcolm first because he was hurt badly enough to hate the help but not badly enough to refuse it. Thorne climbed along the underside from the other trailer like a man repairing a bridge in a storm. Eddie kept shouting status from the Explorer. The tyrannosaurs pushed, pulled, paused, returned. Each impact made the trailer slide another inch. Kelly helped Arby upward. Arby kept saying, “I’m okay.” He was not okay. Adults said fine. Arby said okay. Same disease. Sa

title: Chapter 15: Dodgson Comes Ashore
text: It had been one hour since Lewis Dodgson decided the island was useful. Useful did not mean safe. Dodgson had never confused the two. Safety was a managerial word, a word for forms and hallway posters. Useful was better. Useful meant the risk had a price attached. Useful meant a man could calculate. The jungle did not make calculation easy. Baselton complained in academic gasps. King kept looking behind them. The local boatmen had refused to stay, which Dodgson considered superstition until the first distant roar rolled through the trees and even he stopped walking for three seconds. Then he continued. “We are close,” he said. King wiped rain from his face. “Close to what?” “A nesting zone.” “How do you know?” Dodgson tapped the plastic map case. “InGen knew where it kept its assets.” Baselton, despite himself, smiled. “Assets. That’s rather good.” King did not smile. Howard King had worked with Dodgson long enough to recognize when a word had crossed a line. Assets meant eggs. Animals. Embryos. Things with teeth and mothers and no consent. But King had also worked with venture money long enough to know that moral discomfort rarely survived contact with patents. He told himself this was a retrieval mission. That was the phrase Dodgson had used on the boat, and it had worked better than theft because retrieval implied prior ownership. A company had made the animals. Another company could acquire their future. The island, inconveniently, kept refusing the grammar. They found the first nest in a low clearing near a stand of cycads. Not tyrannosaur. Smaller. Several shallow depressions. Broken shell fragments. Tracks like knives in mud. Dodgson knelt. “Velociraptor.” King stepped back. Baselton whispered, “Good God.” Dodgson opened the cooler. Taking eggs was simple in theory. In theory, the adult animals ranged away to hunt. In theory, eggs were inert biological material. In theory, time on the ground could be kept below four minutes. In theory, no animal understood th

title: Chapter 16: The Wrong Kind of Sample
text: It had been thirteen minutes since they counted everyone and came up short. Arby was gone. The sentence would not hold still in Kelly’s mind. It kept changing shape. Arby was in the hide. Arby was in the grass. Arby was taken. Arby was hiding. Arby was dead. Arby was not dead because Kelly had not permitted the world to do that, and surely the world still required permission for the worst things. Sarah grabbed her shoulders. “Look at me.” Kelly looked. “Breathe once.” “We have to—” “Breathe once.” Kelly breathed. “Good. Now tell me exactly where you saw him last.” Practical questions saved her. Sarah knew that. Kelly did not have to feel if she was reporting. She gave the sequence: trailer tilt, rope, mud, confusion, Malcolm, Levine, the radio, empty space. Thorne listened with a face that made him look older by ten years. Eddie checked the high hide feed. Dead. “We go now,” Kelly said. “We go with a plan,” Sarah said. “That’s adult for later.” “No. That’s alive for later.” Malcolm pushed himself up on one elbow. “The Velociraptors took him for a reason.” Kelly turned on him. “Don’t make this a lecture.” He accepted that. Quietly. It was the first thing he had accepted all night. “Not a lecture,” Malcolm said. “A clue.” The clue was the cage. Levine had brought a collapsible observation cage for small animals. It had been loaded near the hide. Arby, in the chaos, had been near it. Velociraptors did not carry boys gently through forests. They did carry objects. They carried food. They carried stolen things back to a place where the pack could examine them without interference. “Nest,” Sarah said. No one liked hearing it. “Then we go to the nest,” Thorne said. “Not all of us.” Kelly said, “I am going.” “No.” “I’m going.” Sarah looked at her for a long second. “You will do exactly what I say.” Thorne said, “Sarah.” “She knows him. He may answer her voice. And she is small enough to move if we have to get through brush.” “I don’t like it.” “I didn’t ask you to like it.”

title: Chapter 17: No Mothers
text: It had been twenty seconds since Arby tried to apologize. No one had time to accept. Sarah hauled him up. Kelly got under his other arm. Arby made one broken sound and then bit it off. That was worse than shouting. Shouting meant the pain had somewhere to go. Arby’s pain stayed in his body and made his knees fail. Kelly hated him for not screaming. The hate lasted less than a second and was not really hate. It was fear looking for a shape. If he screamed, he would be hurt in a normal way. If he stayed quiet, the damage was too big to name. Thorne reversed the Explorer toward them. Eddie leaned out the passenger side with the rifle. “Move!” The Velociraptors came from three directions. They did not rush blindly. That was the detail Kelly would remember later. They paused at the edges of light, adjusted to the vehicle, to the humans, to the wounded boy, to the fallen branch, to the animal hit by the dart. Their heads moved in small exact cuts. They were solving. Sarah pushed Arby toward the open door. “Kelly, in.” “You first.” “In.” Kelly climbed because Sarah’s voice had become a tool. Arby fell across the seat. Eddie grabbed him. Sarah slammed the door as the first Velociraptor hit the side panel hard enough to dent it inward. Thorne floored the accelerator. The Explorer leapt backward. They did not escape. They relocated the chase. Velociraptors ran beside the vehicle, low and smooth, appearing in flashes between grass and mud. One jumped onto the hood and slid across the glass, claws screaming against metal. Eddie shouted. Thorne swerved. The animal fell away and another took its place at the side mirror, jaws closing on plastic and steel. Kelly held Arby’s head in her lap. “Stay awake.” “I am awake.” “Say something.” “Something.” “Not funny.” “Wasn’t trying.” His eyes kept sliding sideways. Kelly pinched his shoulder hard enough to make him flinch. “Ow.” “Good.” “You are terrible at bedside manner.” “Stay alive and file a complaint.” Blood ran from his mouth wher

title: Chapter 18: The Tyrannosaur Pair
text: It had been two tries since the Velociraptor learned the handle. On the third try, Eddie shot the door. The dart punched through the upper panel and vanished outside. The handle stopped moving. Something hissed. Claws scraped metal, then withdrew. No one cheered. Cheering belonged to endings. This was only a pause. Sarah finished closing Arby’s wounds. He had lost teeth, blood, and the last of his childhood belief that adults could keep categories separate. Kelly sat beside him, one hand on his wrist, feeling the pulse because Sarah told her to and because the pulse kept proving the world had not taken him. Every pulse felt like a small argument won. Kelly counted them in groups of ten. At thirty, Arby opened one swollen eye. “Stop counting out loud.” “I’m not.” “Your face is.” Malcolm lay against the wall, sweating. His leg was worse. Sarah knew it. Thorne knew it. Malcolm knew they knew it and resented the math. “We need to leave the village,” Sarah said. “How?” Eddie asked. The answer came from outside. A tyrannosaur called in the valley, long and low. Another answered. The Velociraptors around the trailer went silent. Malcolm closed his eyes. “Interesting hierarchy.” “Ian,” Sarah said. “Yes?” “Try not to admire the food chain right now.” The tyrannosaurs saved them by wanting something else. They moved through the lower edge of the village in the blue before dawn, enormous and half-seen, following scent. The Velociraptors withdrew without drama. One moment the trailer was surrounded by clicks and soft movement. The next it was alone with larger footsteps. That was the first mercy the island had given them, and even mercy had teeth. Levine watched through the rear slit window. “They are searching for the juvenile.” “The one you contaminated us with?” Eddie said. Levine did not respond. Good, Kelly thought. Maybe shame had finally found him. Thorne used the silence. He got them moving. Not far. The Explorer was damaged. The trailer had one good wheel assembly maki

title: Chapter 19: The Trailer Over the Edge
text: It had been one hour since the trailer went over the edge. Now they had to admit what had gone with it. Food. Water. Medical stock. The stronger radio array. Most of Eddie’s tools. Two spare battery modules. One equipment case marked FIELD SYSTEMS that Eddie looked at from the cliff top as if it had contained a relative. The wreck lay below them in the green. Close enough to see. Too far to use. Jack Thorne hated that distance. Engineers hated the unusable. A broken thing could be repaired. A lost thing could be replaced. A thing visible and unreachable was an insult dressed as geography. “We can climb down,” Eddie said. “No,” Sarah said. “We need the gear.” “We need you alive.” Eddie looked at Thorne for appeal. Thorne looked at the slope, the mud, the torn trees, the prints already appearing near the wreck. “No,” he said. Eddie turned away. Kelly watched him because Eddie’s disappointment frightened her. Adults angry at danger were one thing. Adults grieving equipment meant the equipment had mattered more than she understood. They inventoried what remained. One damaged Explorer. One damaged trailer. One motorcycle. Two dart rifles with limited cartridges. Three injured people, depending on how honest the count was. Malcolm badly. Arby moderately. Sarah enough to ignore it. Everyone else cut, bruised, exhausted, and pretending those did not qualify. One corporate thief with stolen eggs. One island that had begun rearranging itself around their mistakes. Dodgson was gone. The tyrannosaur had driven him off the road and into the trees, but no one had seen the end of it. Sarah had recovered the cooler where he dropped it. Inside were three eggs wrapped in foam and greed. Levine stared at them. “We have to return those.” “No,” Sarah said. “If the adults track scent—” “No.” “Sarah, listen to me.” She slammed the cooler lid shut. “I am listening to you. That is the problem.” The eggs were not returned because the nest was not safe, and because return was a human story. T

title: Chapter 20: A Rope in the Rain
text: It had been three minutes since the invisible animals became visible. Then invisible again. That was the part Eddie hated. A charging animal was a problem with direction. A hidden animal was a problem with imagination, and imagination made too much noise inside the head. They barricaded the storehouse. Thorne shoved a glass-front refrigerator against the door. Eddie stacked shelves behind it. Sarah checked windows. Levine found flashlights and became briefly useful by removing batteries from dead units and matching them with working ones. Kelly opened cans. Arby sat on the counter under protest, holding a flashlight and a screwdriver like a tiny judge. Outside, the Carnotaurus moved only when the lights crossed them. The beam would touch leaves. Then the leaves would have a chest. A shoulder. A head. An eye. Then the animal would change again and the beam would find only green. Levine was horrified into admiration. “Chromatophores at this complexity…” Sarah said, “Richard.” “Yes?” “If you say beautiful, I will put you outside with a mirror.” He swallowed the word. The storehouse became night before night came. They turned off most lights to preserve power and because light made them visible. Rain started again. It hit the roof in waves, first soft, then hard, then hard enough that speech had to lean close. Malcolm drifted in and out of fever. When he woke, he talked. Not always to them. “The great human fantasy,” he said, eyes half closed, “is that knowledge improves behavior.” Sarah checked his bandage. “Ian.” “No, really. We learn the stove is hot. Then we build a hotter stove. We learn a cliff is dangerous. Then we sell tickets to the view.” “Fever.” “Probably. But accurate fever.” Kelly listened from the counter. She had never heard anyone insult the entire species while bleeding on canned peaches. It should have been ridiculous. It was, a little. But outside the window, something large and almost invisible breathed against the bars, and Malcolm’s fever began to

title: Chapter 21: The Nest Smell
text: It had been one crawl-space turn since Kelly saw the tracks. She did not shout. That was her first victory. She stopped. Arby bumped into her ankle. Behind him, Sarah whispered for status. Kelly kept her light on the track. Three toes. Deep claw points. Mud not dry. Something had used the tunnel recently, and something might still be using it ahead. “Track,” Kelly whispered. The message passed backward in pieces. Track. Fresh. Stop. Sarah came forward over Arby with a motion that should not have been possible in the narrow passage. She looked at the print, then smelled the air. Kelly saw her face change before Sarah spoke. “Nest smell.” No one asked what that meant. The tunnel had become too small for questions. Behind them, the storehouse door crashed. The barricade held, but not with conviction. Ahead, the tunnel sloped down. Sarah listened. Water drip. Metal ping. Distant engine hum from some old system still refusing to die. And beneath that, a soft scratch. They could not go back. That was the island’s favorite lesson. Sarah took first position. Kelly moved behind her. Arby behind Kelly. The others followed in a slow painful chain: Malcolm dragged and helped, Thorne pushing, Eddie carrying the kit, Levine muttering apologies to the concrete whenever he hit his shoulder. The tunnel opened into a utility chamber. Cable racks lined the walls. Old fans hung dead behind mesh. A maintenance ladder rose to a trapdoor. In the corner, leaves and shredded insulation had been pulled into a mound. Empty. Mostly. Sarah held up one fist. Everyone stopped. In the mound were bones. Small bones, large bones, plastic fragments, and a strip of cloth that might have been from an old uniform. The smell was heavy, warm, sour. Velociraptors had nested here. Not now, perhaps. But recently enough that the room still belonged to them. Arby began to shake. Kelly took his hand. He whispered, “I’m okay.” “Stop lying.” “Okay.” From above came the sound of the Carnotaurus striking the storeh

title: Chapter 22: Eddie Carr's Last Repair
text: It had been forty minutes since the Tyrannosaurus rex took Dodgson. Eddie Carr repaired the Explorer because machines, unlike people, still occasionally answered reason. The vehicle sat nose-down near the road depression. One battery coupling had shaken loose. The left drive assembly was packed with mud. The side panel hung by a wire. The radio antenna was bent but not dead. Eddie lay under it with rain dripping into his ear and told the car exactly what he thought of it. The car did not argue. That helped. Above him, Thorne kept watch with the rifle. Sarah watched the opposite tree line. Kelly and Arby sat inside because Sarah ordered them to, though Kelly kept the door open and one foot out, ready to disobey in the direction of usefulness. Malcolm slept or fainted in the back. Levine pretended to read the map but mostly watched Sarah, perhaps waiting to be forgiven by someone who had no current budget for forgiveness. “How long?” Thorne asked. “Longer if you ask again.” “Eddie.” “Five minutes if the coupling seats. Never if it doesn’t.” “Good.” “That is not good.” “It’s a number.” Eddie smiled despite himself, then lost the smile when the wrench slipped. Sarah heard the herd before she saw it. She raised a hand. Everything stopped. Through the trees came heavy breathing, water slosh, low grunts. Not predators. Too many feet. Too much body. Sarah moved to the road edge and saw green dome-headed animals clustered around a mudhole below, drinking and pushing at one another with heavy skulls. “Pachycephalosaurs,” Levine whispered, unable to stop himself. “Temperament?” Sarah asked. “Unclear.” Sarah watched one slam its domed head into another with a crack like timber splitting. “Getting clearer.” The herd blocked the road ahead. Eddie, under the Explorer, said, “Please tell me they are small.” “No,” Kelly said from the window. “Please tell me they are friendly.” “Also no.” The battery coupling seated with a hard click. Eddie froze. Then he laughed once, short and wild

title: Chapter 23: The Grass Begins to Move
text: It had been five minutes since the radio promised a helicopter. Promises made by radios were fragile. Everyone knew it. No one said it. The Explorer climbed the ridge road with the damaged trailer left behind and the motorcycle tied badly to the rear frame. The vehicle had become less a machine than a collection of temporary agreements. Eddie listened to every sound. Thorne drove by feel. Sarah watched the grass. The grass moved wrong. Wind moved grass in sheets. These lines cut against it. Three on the left. Two on the right. Maybe more hidden by terrain. Velociraptors did not need to show themselves now. They had learned the vehicle’s speed. They had learned its damage. They had learned that injured animals rode inside. Kelly sat with one hand on Arby’s shoulder. “You still awake?” “I wish I wasn’t.” “Correct answer.” Malcolm opened one eye. “The children are becoming field scientists.” Sarah said, “Don’t encourage them.” “I would never. Field science is mostly discomfort plus regret.” The first Velociraptor hit from the left. Not the vehicle. The motorcycle strapped behind it. The bike snapped sideways, dragging the rear frame. Eddie cursed and reached for the release. “Don’t!” Thorne said. “It’s pulling us.” “If it drops, they get ahead.” Sarah was already opening the rear hatch. Kelly grabbed Sarah’s belt without being told. Sarah looked back once. “Good.” That single word hit Kelly harder than praise. It was not kindness. It was assessment. She had done the correct thing. In Sarah Harding’s world, correct mattered more than good. Sarah cut the loose strap, not the main one. The motorcycle swung, slammed into the side, and knocked the Velociraptor away. Eddie released the second clamp. Thorne swerved. The bike dropped under the wheels of the pursuing animals in a crash of metal and animal screams. The Explorer lurched free. The grass behind them erupted, then fell back. “That bought seconds,” Sarah said. “How many?” Eddie asked. “Enough to spend badly.” The roa

title: Chapter 24: The Storehouse
text: It had been ninety seconds since the helicopter left. No one spoke because speech would have made it official. The north clearing was empty. Rotor wash had flattened the wet grass in a clean circle. A crate strap lay in the mud. The air still smelled of aviation fuel. The helicopter had been there. The helicopter had waited. The helicopter had decided the island was not worth another minute. Eddie got out first. “No.” He said it to the clearing. The clearing accepted it without interest. Thorne called on the radio until his voice roughened. Static answered. Once, a distant Spanish voice broke through, too fast and too frightened. Then nothing. Sarah stood at the edge of the clearing, looking at the sky. Malcolm stayed in the vehicle. His face had gone gray. Kelly wanted to blame someone. The pilot. Dodgson. Levine. The tree. The Velociraptors. Herself. Blame moved quickly because it had nowhere useful to go. Arby said, “There’s another way.” Everyone turned. His voice was weak but certain. “The map showed river access. North tunnel to river dock. If the old power grid still has doors active, we can get there from the storehouse system.” Levine blinked. “The storehouse we left?” Arby nodded. Eddie looked at the road behind them. In the fog, the grass began to move again. Thorne got back into the Explorer. “Then we go back.” Going back felt like losing. Kelly had never realized direction could have morality. Forward meant brave. Back meant failure. But Isla Sorna did not care which way a human face pointed. The road back to the storehouse was the same road, only now the Velociraptors knew more about them. Sarah said they would not outrun the pack forever. Malcolm said nothing, which meant he agreed. Thorne drove with the last of the battery. At the fallen tree gap, the Velociraptors attacked again. This time from above. One dropped from an overhanging branch onto the roof with a metallic crash. Claws punched into the solar panel. Eddie made a sound that combined grief

title: Chapter 25: Carnotaurus in the Dark
text: It had been one second since the lights went out. The dark was not empty. Kelly knew that before the flashlight came on. Empty dark had a shape. Bedroom dark. Movie theater dark. Closet dark. This dark had breath in it. Eddie switched on a flashlight. The beam found concrete walls, pipes, Malcolm’s pale face, Sarah’s rifle, Levine’s eyes too wide. Then the beam found a pattern on the wall. Leaves. But there were no leaves underground. Sarah said, “Light down.” Eddie lowered the beam. The pattern shifted. A Carnotaurus stood in the tunnel twenty yards ahead, body pressed almost flat to the wall, skin wearing the concrete shadow like clothing. Its head turned toward them. Two small horns caught the light. No one moved. The animal did not attack. That was worse. Behind them, the sealed door shook as Velociraptors struck from the storehouse side. Ahead, the Carnotaurus blocked the route to the river. Malcolm whispered, “The island is becoming repetitive.” Sarah whispered, “Quiet.” Sarah did not shoot. The tunnel was too narrow. The animal too close. If it charged after being hit, it would crush them before the venom worked. If it did not charge, they still had to pass it. Field problems were always uglier than range problems. Arby, from behind Kelly, whispered, “Lights.” “What?” “The tunnel lights. I can trigger sections from the panel if the local grid is still responding.” “Panel is behind the door.” “No. The portable.” He held up the small maintenance tablet he had taken from the storehouse terminal rack. Kelly stared at him. “You stole that?” “Borrowed.” “Arby.” “Useful.” He tapped the dead screen. Nothing happened. Velociraptors hit the door again. Eddie’s flashlight shook. Arby opened the back of the tablet, pulled the battery, wiped the contacts on his shirt, and reseated it. The screen flickered. Kelly almost kissed him on the forehead and decided not to embarrass both of them during a Carnotaurus incident. Sarah kept her eyes on the animal. “What can you do?” “

title: Chapter 26: Kelly Counts the Seconds
text: It had been eight seconds since the Carnotaurus hit the door. Kelly counted because counting was smaller than fear. Nine. Ten. The door held. Eleven. It hit again. Twelve. The frame cracked. “Down!” Sarah shouted. They carried Malcolm down the concrete stairs toward the sound of water. The stairwell smelled of rust and river mud. Old emergency lights glowed red in cages along the wall. Some worked. Some flickered. Some showed only the dead bulbs inside, useless little moons. Arby stumbled. Kelly caught him. “I have you.” “I know.” “Say something.” “Something.” “Still not funny.” “Still wasn’t trying.” At the bottom of the stairs was a service dock carved into the volcanic rock. Dark river water moved through a tunnel, black and fast, reflecting strips of red light. Two boats were tied there. One had no engine. The other had an engine. No one trusted that coincidence. Eddie and Thorne went to the working boat. “Battery,” Eddie said. “Fuel?” “Electric auxiliary. Maybe charged from the grid.” “Maybe?” “Maybe is the official language of this island.” Sarah positioned everyone. Malcolm on the boat. Arby beside him. Kelly with the medical kit. Levine forward with a pole because Sarah no longer cared whether his hands were made for tenure. King and Baselton had vanished somewhere behind the chaos; no one had the strength to search. Dodgson was gone in a way that required no paperwork. The door at the top of the stairs failed. The sound came down to them as metal folding. Kelly began counting again. One. Thorne untied the bow line. Two. Eddie opened the engine casing. Three. Sarah aimed up the stairs. Four. Claws hit concrete. Five. “Eddie,” Thorne said. “I know.” Six. The first Velociraptor appeared above them. Not Carnotaurus. Worse in that moment. Smaller. Faster. Able to use stairs. Sarah fired. Seven. The animal fell against the railing. Eight. Another came over it. Kelly picked up a flare from the emergency box. She did not ask. She struck it. White fire filled the do

title: Chapter 27: Arby's Door
text: It had been six minutes since Kelly saw the smoke. Smoke meant people. That was the old rule. On Isla Sorna, smoke meant people had been there recently enough to leave evidence, which might mean rescue, fire, trap, engine trouble, or Lewis Dodgson somehow surviving out of pure corporate spite. Kelly had stopped trusting old rules. The boat drifted downriver with the motor low. Thorne steered toward the bank. Eddie watched the fuel gauge as if hostility could fill it. Sarah watched the trees. Malcolm lay under a wet blanket, eyes closed, lips moving sometimes. Levine sat forward with the pole, newly silent. Arby leaned against Kelly, awake but gray. “You okay?” Kelly whispered. “Stop asking.” “Stop bleeding.” “I’m trying.” The smoke came from a utility dock. Not a rescue boat. Not a camp. A small electrical fire in a junction box near a locked service gate. The gate blocked a ramp up to the river road. Beyond it, through vines, they could see the shape of a maintenance vehicle under a tarp. Last vehicle, maybe. Last chance, maybe. The gate had a keypad. Of course it did. Arby looked at the keypad and sighed. “No.” Kelly said, “No one said anything.” “You all looked at me.” Thorne crouched by the panel. “Can you open it?” Arby gave him a look that was almost Malcolm’s. “That is saying something.” The keypad was dead. The access box beneath it was not. It had an old maintenance port and a manual override sealed behind a plate. Eddie removed the plate. Arby connected the portable tablet with cables that did not fit until Eddie made them fit by shaving insulation with a knife. Sarah stood guard at the ramp. The jungle above them clicked. Kelly did not say anything. She had learned that not every sound needed witnesses. Arby worked. His fingers were swollen. Twice he hit the wrong key and hissed at himself. Kelly wanted to tell him it was fine. It was not fine. Fine was not useful. Instead she held the cable steady. “You’ve got it,” she said. “You don’t know that.” “No. B

title: Chapter 28: Malcolm Stops Explaining
text: It had been two seconds since the gate betrayed them. Eddie got the utility truck started on the third try. Kelly would remember that forever because the first try made nothing happen, the second made smoke, and the third made the engine produce a grinding hum so beautiful that Eddie actually kissed the steering wheel. “Later,” Sarah said. “Right.” They loaded without order. Malcolm across the rear. Arby beside him. Kelly on the floor. Levine half-hanging from the side until Thorne yanked him in. Sarah last, firing twice through the back as Eddie drove. The truck did not accelerate. It reconsidered. Then it moved. Velociraptors came through the gate and hit the ramp behind them. One jumped onto the truck bed. Sarah drove the rifle stock into its head, shoved, and fired point blank when it came back up. The animal fell away. Another took its place. Malcolm watched from the floor, eyes fever-bright. “They are improvising.” Sarah slammed another cartridge in. “Ian.” “What?” “Stop explaining.” For once, he did. The road from the river dock led through the oldest part of Site B. Not the showy industrial complex. Not the village. Older service buildings, half-collapsed sheds, storage tanks, pipes running along the ground, warning signs bleached by weather. InGen had built layers of intention on the island. Each layer failed and was covered by vines. Each failure remained underneath, waiting to trip the living. Eddie drove around a sinkhole. The truck’s rear wheel clipped the edge. For one horrible second the vehicle tilted toward the drop. Everyone leaned without being told. The truck came back down. Levine exhaled in a high, thin sound. Kelly began to laugh again and stopped because Arby was watching her with concern. “I’m fine.” “You keep saying that.” “Now you know how annoying it is.” He almost smiled. Malcolm did not. His eyes were closed, but his hand moved against the floor, fingers tapping a rhythm only he could hear. Sarah checked him between shots. Fever. Blood

title: Chapter 29: Radio Silence
text: It had been thirty seconds since the river took them. The boathouse vanished behind a bend. No one trusted that. They stayed low in the boat. Eddie kept one hand on the engine housing as if physical affection might prevent failure. Thorne steered. Sarah held pressure on Malcolm’s leg. Kelly held pressure on Arby’s shoulder, though Arby kept saying that wound had stopped bleeding, which was exactly the sort of thing a wound would want him to think. The radio was dead. Not weak. Not static. Dead. Thorne tried every channel. Emergency. Field. Local. Marine. Old InGen service band. The handset produced only a click and the small useless hiss of electronics talking to themselves. “Antenna’s gone,” Eddie said. “Can you fix it?” “From a moving boat with no parts?” “Yes.” Eddie looked at him. Thorne said, “I accept that was optimistic.” Radio silence changed the journey. Before, rescue had been a thing that might be called. Now rescue was geography. Find the coast. Find a settlement. Find any boat not owned by a man who collected eggs. It also changed the people in the boat. When there was a radio, everyone could pretend the next voice would be adult enough for all of them. A pilot. A harbor official. A coast guard officer. Some unseen person with a clipboard, a working engine, and authority. Without the radio, authority shrank to the few square feet of wet deck around their shoes. Thorne’s hands on the wheel. Sarah’s hands on Malcolm’s bandage. Eddie’s ear pressed near the motor housing. Arby’s eyes half-open, trying to stay conscious because Kelly kept ordering him to. The old world had not vanished. It had simply stopped answering. Kelly looked at the dead handset and hated it with a personal hate. She hated its black plastic, its coiled cord, its little speaker full of nothing. It was worse than a broken door because a broken door at least admitted it was wood and metal. The radio had made a promise by existing. Arby shifted against her. “You’re squeezing.” Kelly looked

title: Chapter 30: The Last Vehicle
text: It had been one rope jerk since Eddie disappeared. Thorne pulled hard enough to hurt himself. The rope came tight, then angled under the boat. Sarah grabbed it with him. Levine moved next, then Kelly, because waiting had become impossible. Arby woke to the motion and tried to sit up. “Stay down,” Kelly snapped. He stayed down. The rope jerked again. Then Eddie surfaced on the far side of the boat, gasping, one hand full of shredded weeds. “Other side!” he coughed. Everyone shouted at once. Eddie spat river water. “Intake clear.” Thorne looked like he might kill him and hug him in the same motion. “Why did the rope pull?” “Caught on the prop bracket.” “You could have said that.” “Underwater?” “Learn.” Eddie climbed back in, shaking and triumphant. The engine started on the first try. For three seconds, Eddie was the happiest man on Isla Sorna. Then something large moved in the mangroves behind them. Happiness ended. The last vehicle was not the boat. It was not even much of a vehicle. From the river, the shed looked like another patch of decay. Rusted roof. Vines through the side wall. A fuel sign hanging by one bolt and tapping in the wind. Eddie saw the tires before anyone else saw the shape. That was how his mind worked. People saw places. Eddie saw what could still roll. “There,” he said. Thorne followed his finger. “That?” “Don’t insult it. It can hear you.” “It is under a tarp.” “That’s dignity.” Sarah looked toward the mangroves behind them. The thing moving there had not shown itself. That made her want distance more than certainty. “We have one minute,” she said. Eddie was already climbing out. They found it at the river mouth. A small service jeep sat under a collapsing canopy near a fuel shed, tires cracked, windshield filmed with salt, doors missing. It had probably belonged to dock maintenance. It had probably not moved in years. It looked like the kind of machine a sensible man would ignore. Eddie loved it on sight. “No,” Sarah said. “Look at it.” “I am

title: Chapter 31: The River Out
text: It had been one command since everyone began to run. Run did not mean the same thing for each of them. For Sarah, run meant controlled descent, eyes ahead, feet choosing ground. For Eddie, run meant half-carrying the medical pack and half-dragging Levine when he stumbled. For Thorne, run meant Malcolm’s weight cutting into his shoulder with every step. For Kelly, run meant Arby’s hand in hers and no permission to fall. Behind them, the Velociraptors came down the ridge. Not in a mob. In a pattern. Two wide. Three center. Others hidden in brush. Sarah saw the pattern and knew the center animals were not the attack. They were pressure. The flanks were the teeth. “Left!” she shouted. Everyone veered left. A Velociraptor burst from the right brush into the space where Arby would have been. Kelly did not think. She threw the metal water bottle in her hand. It struck the animal’s snout. Not hard enough to hurt. Hard enough to insult. The Velociraptor snapped toward her. Sarah hit it with the empty rifle like a staff. “Keep moving!” The dock boards were slick. The boat was real. That mattered more than its condition. It had a cabin. It had fuel drums. It had an engine covered with a tarp. It had ropes, life jackets, flares, tools, and the smell of men who expected to return. Eddie and Thorne got Malcolm aboard. Sarah got Arby. Kelly jumped after him and landed badly, pain flashing up her ankle. She crawled anyway. Levine threw the mooring rope onto the deck and then climbed after it with surprising speed. The first Velociraptor reached the dock. Eddie was at the engine. “Start!” Thorne shouted. “I am aware of the narrative need!” The engine turned. Failed. Turned. Failed. Sarah took a flare, struck it, and threw it onto the dock. Fire burst between boat and pack. The Velociraptors recoiled, but not far. Their heads moved, reading the flame, the gaps, the people, the boat. Kelly pulled Arby into the cabin. On the wall was an emergency radio. “Arby.” “I see it.” “Can you?” “

title: Chapter 32: Extinction Has a Pattern
text: It had been twenty minutes since the island became distance. Distance did not make it smaller. Not yet. Isla Sorna sat behind them under cloud, green and volcanic and impossible, still large enough to fill the rear window. The boat moved through choppy water toward a rescue vessel that had not yet appeared. The radio worked. The engine worked. Malcolm’s fever did not improve. Sarah sat beside him. Every few minutes she checked his pulse with two fingers against his throat. Every few minutes he opened one eye as if this offended him personally. “Still alive,” she said. “A humiliatingly empirical conclusion.” “Hold still.” “I am lying down in a stolen boat after being bitten by bad corporate judgment. Stillness is my main contribution.” The joke cost him. Sarah saw the breath he had to take afterward. She did not call him on it. He knew she knew. That was enough cruelty for one room. Levine sat opposite with the InGen memos spread on his knees, though the pages were damp and smeared and mostly unreadable now. He kept returning to the feed notes. Rendered protein. Sheep extract. Carnivore supplement. Halt exogenous protein. Disease cannot continue. “Prions,” Sarah said. Levine looked up. “Protein fragments,” she said. “Misfolded. Infectious without being alive. If they fed contaminated animal protein into carnivores…” “And then scavengers,” Malcolm said, eyes closed. Levine leaned forward. “You think that explains the instability?” “I think,” Malcolm said, “that humans built a system too complex to understand, then fed it leftovers and called the result nature.” No one spoke for a moment. Outside, the sea struck the hull. Malcolm kept talking. That frightened Sarah because he had stopped explaining before. Now the explanation came out slow, stripped of performance. “Extinction is not a meteor hitting a clean target. It is not one dramatic cause. It is a system losing resilience. Stress here, disease there, behavior disrupted, social structure missing, food chain distor

title: Chapter 33: Leaving Site B
text: It had been five minutes since rescue became hands. Hands pulled them across. That was how leaving began. Not with music. Not with triumph. Hands gripping wrists, shoulders, straps, stretchers. Spanish commands. Blankets thrown over wet bodies. A medic taking one look at Malcolm and shouting for a kit. Eddie refusing help until his knees made their own decision. Arby being lifted as if he weighed nothing. Kelly hating that and loving it. Sarah crossed last. She stood on the deck of the rescue vessel and looked back at the damaged boat. It bumped against the hull, already becoming an object instead of a place. The deck where the Velociraptor had landed. The cabin where Malcolm had explained extinction. The radio Arby had used. The last small room between them and the island. “Dr. Harding?” The medic wanted her hand. Sarah gave it to him. Only then did she notice how badly it hurt. The questions began immediately. Names. Injuries. Number of people. Who else was on the island. Were there bodies. Was there contamination. What animals. What company. What coordinates. Thorne answered what mattered. Sarah answered what saved time. Malcolm answered nothing because the medic had sedated him and the world was briefly improved. Levine tried to answer everything. Kelly watched Sarah stop him with one look. Evidence, not spectacle. That became the rule. They sealed the memos in plastic. They sealed the tissue samples Sarah had taken from Malcolm’s bandages and the mud on Dodgson’s cooler. They wrote coordinates. They listed hazards. They used exact species names where possible and uncertainty where honest. That part mattered to Sarah. Not monster. Not beast. Not ancient terror. Animal. Juvenile. Adult. Defensive. Predatory. Social. Observed behavior under stress. Evidence of learning. Evidence of parental care. Evidence of abnormal feeding history. Every word that made the island less myth made it harder for someone else to sell the myth back to the world. Arby insisted on writi

title: Chapter 34: What Survives
text: It had been three days since they left Isla Sorna. The mainland tried to make the island smaller. It used white sheets, hospital bracelets, official forms, clean hallways, plastic cups of water, men in suits, women with clipboards, translated statements, and the heavy silence of organizations deciding which truth would be least expensive. It used phrases like maritime incident, unauthorized biological preserve, restricted corporate property, and traumatic misperception. Kelly hated traumatic misperception most. She had seen a Velociraptor learn a door handle. No phrase in a suit could make that smaller. Arby sat beside her in the hospital waiting room with two missing teeth, one bandaged eyebrow, and a laptop balanced on his knees. He was not supposed to have the laptop. That had stopped meaning much. “What are you doing?” “Copying the notes.” “Sarah said evidence, not spectacle.” “This is evidence.” “Looks like hacking.” “Evidence sometimes has a keyboard.” Kelly leaned closer. On the screen were maps, access paths, camera IDs, and a directory named SITEB_ARCHIVE_RECOVERED. “Arby.” “What?” “Make two copies.” He smiled without showing teeth. The copier in the nurses’ station made a sound every time it finished a page. Kelly began counting without meaning to. One. Two. Three. Each page felt too small for what it carried. Maps on cheap paper. Lab directories in black ink. Notes typed by people who had gone home years ago and never wondered whether the things they made were still hungry. “Three copies,” she said. Arby looked at her. “One for someone official,” she said. “One for someone competent. One for us.” He considered that with solemn professional respect. “That’s actually good protocol.” “I know.” Levine gave his statement twice. The first time, it was too long. Sarah told him so. title: Chapter 1: The Saturday Error
story: Jurassic Park IV: The Judas Strain
text: “Run, Joshua, run.” Eli’s mother had a voice that carried. She was hollering. Joshua was up. Joshua was always up at the wrong time, in her telling, and the wrong time was every time the cooler was empty and Benjamin was in his book and Rebecca was on the bench beside her trying to eat a cheese cube with the wrong end of a plastic fork. Behind her, the Video Enthusiast had his shoulder hunched into the camera as if it were a rifle. He had paid serious money for the unit and it deserved serious posture. Out on the mound the pitcher kicked dirt at nothing. Right field belonged to Timmy, and Timmy belonged to no one. He stood with his mitt open the way you hold a bag for groceries. His father was on the bleachers with his face in his hands. The parents kept score harder than the kids. Runs. Base runners. Minutes until the cooler could come out. The aluminum bleachers were hot under thighs. The metal smelled of iron and sunscreen and the inside of a hand. A man two rows up was on a phone call about a refrigerator. A woman with a clipboard kept score in the careful pencil of a former librarian. The ball came off the bat with a clink that meant nothing, a dribbler between first and second, and Joshua’s mother went into a litany of run the way she did every Saturday, and Joshua ran, and the shortstop was waiting on second by the time the throw arrived, and Joshua had his hands on his hips at the bag before the second baseman had even turned to look. “He got a double,” she said. “A single and an error.” Benjamin did not look up from his book. The pitcher’s father said something about flu season. The coach told the pitcher to get back on the mound. Yappy Dog remembered it. Yappy Dog had been yapping since the parking lot opened. Yappy Dog stopped yapping. The leash was tight. The stake held. The small dog’s spine had gone rigid the way small dogs go rigid when something larger has come into their air. He looked up. title: Chapter 2: The Aviary Exile
story: Jurassic Park IV: The Judas Strain
text: The Speiler man on the television had a Swiss accent he was trying to flatten into something a Midwestern audience would forgive, and Nick Harris sat at the bar with his thumb pressed to his glass and listened to him try. “Our eradication of the genetically engineered creatures at the former Jurassic Park is nearly complete.” Speiler had a tie the same color as a corporate folder. “We believe that these current depredations can be attributed to animals that escaped the island before we at Grendel took possession of it.” Nick pushed the glass aside. “Right, buddy.” The bartender was a kid in a cut-off Hooters T-shirt who had not been alive when the original park went down. He looked at the news the way kids looked at the news, which is to say at the chyron, not the man. The chyron said GRENDEL CORP. and below that, in smaller letters, Statement on Continental Migration. That was a lawyer’s word. Migration. Nick had been around enough lawyers to know what the word meant. It meant the company was going to win. He paid in cash and left a dollar on the bar. Outside, the boardwalk was hot and the boards were loose where the dock met the parking. There were teenagers on a bench eating ice cream out of cardboard, and a woman walking a fat black dog, and an old man selling shells from a folding table the way old men did at every beach Nick had ever been to. The air had that flat tin smell of low tide. He squinted against the sun and started walking nowhere in particular. A shadow crossed him. He looked up. Two pelicans, three pelicans, the shapes wrong only in the half-second before the brain caught them and decided they were fine. He let his shoulders drop. Two years of looking up. Two years of being wrong about what was up there. He kept walking. “Nick.” He turned. The man coming up the boardwalk had a quasi-military uniform with a hummingbird patch on the breast pocket, and a gait that was slightly off in both legs. The face was not slightly off.
