title: Chapter 6: Profile Not Found
text: Mateo Valverde ran the wrong way first. That was what saved him from believing the park still made sense. The staff corridor outside the clean-change hallway should have turned left toward the shuttle point. Mateo knew this because he had pushed carts through it for nine days. Left, then the boot wash, then the contractor entrance, then the covered path to east transport. Three minutes if the floor was dry. Four if the floor was wet. Five if a manager stopped him to ask why the bags were not labeled with the new stickers. Mateo turned left. The fire door at the end of the corridor was closed. Not locked. Closed was normal. Closed meant push the bar and move. He hit the bar with both hands. The door did not move. He hit it again. Above the bar, a new red tag hung from the latch, swinging in the air from his impact. SECURITY DEGRADE AUTO-SEAL ACTIVE No one had mentioned auto-seal in orientation. No one mentioned many things. Mateo put Jophery Brown’s badge to the reader. The reader thought for three seconds and gave him a green light. The door still did not move. Green did not mean open. That was the next lesson. Behind him, down the corridor he had come from, a PA speaker popped and said half a sentence. …East Dock vessel departing at nineteen hundred… Then the speaker died. Mateo looked at the red tag. He looked at Jophery’s route sheet. Jophery’s handwriting beside the main corridor said: FIRE DOOR FAILS SHUT WHEN NETWORK DROPS. Under it, in smaller letters: USE LAUNDRY. Mateo turned around. He ran right. The laundry route was not on the guest map. The laundry route was not on the contractor orientation map either. It ran behind the service toilets, past a linen chute, through a room where damp staff uniforms hung on a pipe rack, then into the back of the kitchen corridor and out toward the shuttle point from the wrong side. Mateo had hated that route because the carts caught on the threshold. Now he loved the threshold because no manager had thought to put an auto

title: Chapter 7: Dead Man's Access
text: Mateo Valverde stayed at the fence until the boat light became the same color as the rain. Then he made himself count. One breath for the dock. One breath for the road. One breath for the stupid green letters on the reader behind him. WELCOME BACK, JOPHERY BROWN. He could still smell diesel. The smell hung under the roof of the service awning and made him think the boat might turn around if he waited long enough. It did not. The east water went black. The dock lines slapped the pilings. Somewhere behind the maintenance shed, sheet metal banged once and kept banging, a loose corner working itself free in the storm. Mateo put his own badge to the dock office reader. Red. He put Jophery’s badge to the reader. Green. The lock opened with a clean little click. He hated the sound immediately. It was too polite. It sounded as if the park had been waiting for the dead man and was pleased he had kept his appointment. The dock office was a narrow room with a radio desk, two rain jackets, a corkboard, and a clipboard under a plastic cover. The clipboard said EAST TRANSPORT MANIFEST. Every name had a mark beside it. Security, maintenance, animal containment, food service. At the bottom, under temporary contractors, there were three printed names and three pencil checks. Mateo Valverde was not there. He checked twice because paper sometimes lied badly. This paper lied cleanly. He picked up the radio handset. “East transport, this is Mateo Valverde. Sanitation. I missed the vessel.” The radio clicked. “East transport, do you copy?” Rain hit the roof. The loose sheet metal kept answering from outside. A printer under the desk woke up. It was not a modern printer. It was a beige box with fanfold paper and black tractor holes. It made a dry insect noise and dragged one line into the world. ACCESS EVENT: BROWN, JOPHERY LOCATION: EAST DOCK OFFICE STATUS: ACTIVE Mateo tore the paper off. “No,” he said. The printer gave him another line. ACTIVE PERSONNEL COUNT UPDATED. He looked through

title: Chapter 8: The Last Shuttle
text: The breath came from low down. Mateo did not move his feet. Moving feet made rubber squeak on wet concrete. He had learned that in the raptor bay. Everything loud began as something small a worker forgot to respect. The door had opened only a handspan. Through the gap he saw rain, black leaves, and a pale shape near the ramp. It was not a raptor. Too low. Too round through the shoulder. It sniffed again and pushed its nose against the bottom of the door. Mateo saw small teeth. Not small enough. He backed up one step. The pump room gave him a stupid gift: the stopped motor restarted. The floor jumped under him. The animal outside flinched away from the door. Mateo hit the close button. Nothing happened. Of course. He used the grease pencil from the dock office and marked the door with one word: NO. Then he took the maintenance ladder. The ladder went up to a roof hatch and out into rain so hard it seemed personal. Mateo crawled out, dragged the hatch shut, and lay flat on the gravel roof while the little animal below worried at the door gap. He kept the flare kit under his chest. He kept Jophery’s badge in his fist. When he finally lifted his head, he saw the last shuttle. It sat on the service road fifty meters inland, angled across the yellow line with one headlight pointing at the trees and one at the sky. The driver’s door hung open. Its hazard lights blinked without rhythm, as if one bulb had to think before it remembered. Mateo climbed down the far side of the pump house and ran crouched along the concrete drainage lip. The shuttle smelled of wet vinyl, diesel, and fear. No driver. No passengers. The key was still in the ignition. Mateo laughed once, because his body was stupid and thought that meant luck. He turned the key. The engine coughed, caught for two seconds, and died. He tried again. The engine clicked. The fuel gauge lay flat under E. Someone had left the shuttle with the lights on and the engine running until it had drunk itself empty. The clipboard

title: Chapter 9: Return to Sender
text: Mateo slid under the shuttle bench. It was not bravery. It was not a plan. It was the only place his body found before the step outside became two steps, then three, then the wet sound of something large smelling the open driver’s door. The bench frame pressed into his ribs. A wrench rolled against his knee. He caught it before it could hit the floor. The thing outside put its head into the shuttle. Mateo saw a gray tongue touch the steering wheel. Not the tyrannosaur. Not a raptor. Bigger than the little animal at the pump house, with a heavy snout and eyes set where a cow’s eyes might have been if cows had been built from bad decisions. It huffed once and took the crackers from the seat with the wrapper still on. Mateo held the wrench with both hands. The animal chewed foil. The radio said, “Hello?” The animal lifted its head. Mateo let go of the wrench and grabbed the radio cord. He pulled the handset under the bench and pressed the side button against his chest to muffle it. “Do not answer this channel,” he whispered. “Go quiet.” Static. The animal outside swallowed the crackers and left. Mateo stayed under the bench until his legs shook from staying still. When he crawled out, the radio display had changed. TRANSMISSION ROUTED BROWN, J. / SERVICE MAINTENANCE Below that: RETURN TO SENDER He took the shuttle’s maintenance clipboard and the dead radio handset. The handset had a screw-on plug. Anything with a screw-on plug could be useful if a person was desperate enough. The nearest communications cabinet was marked on Jophery’s route sheet as COM-3. It sat under a tin rain shelter beside the service road, between two trees that had grown too close to the concrete. The cabinet had three locks. Jophery’s badge opened the top lock. The middle lock needed a key. The bottom lock had been broken before Mateo was hired. He opened it with the wrench. Inside were phone junctions, paper tags, dust turned to paste, and a laminated service ticket sealed in a plastic sleeve.

title: Chapter 10: The Park Remembers the Dead
text: The staff break room was behind the maintenance office, past a soda machine with no power and a door that opened for Jophery without asking Mateo anything. Mateo went in because his hands would not stop shaking. He told himself he was looking for batteries. That was mostly true. The break room had a microwave, six plastic chairs, a sink full of mugs, a board of safety notices, and a cork strip crowded with photographs. Men on boats. Women holding babies. A boy in a school uniform with two front teeth missing. A wedding picture folded at the corner. A Polaroid of Tomás Araya standing beside a service cart with both thumbs up, pretending the cart was a race car. Mateo took the Polaroid down and put it in his shirt pocket. Not for a reason he could name. Jophery Brown had three photographs. One showed him in a baseball cap, holding a fish too small for the pride on his face. One showed a woman and two girls at a kitchen table. The youngest girl had birthday cake on her chin. The third photograph was Jophery with three other workers under the raptor service awning. Someone had written on the white border: FIVE MINUTES, BROWN SAID. Mateo did not laugh. He searched Jophery’s cubby below the corkboard. Batteries, a clean pair of socks, a pack of gum, two pay stubs, a folded complaint form. The complaint form was dated six days before Jophery died. It said reader D-17 had opened for the wrong person during a drill. It said the fallback table was older than the live roster. It said temporary and transfer personnel were not being tested under degrade conditions. It said, in Jophery’s careful print: IF THIS FAILS DURING A CONTAINMENT EVENT, SOMEONE WILL BE LOCKED WHERE THEY SHOULD NOT BE. Dale Haskins had signed the bottom. ACKNOWLEDGED. No action line. No repair line. Acknowledged was a word people used when they wanted a problem to die on paper. Mateo put the complaint form with the manifest and the printed access log. Evidence was too strong a word. Evidence belonged to peo

title: Chapter 11: Rex Weather
text: The safest route on Jophery’s sheet ran toward the sound. Mateo did not know what made the sound. Not at first. The building did not shake like a movie building. It shifted in small dishonest ways. Dust came down from pipe clamps. Water rings trembled in a coffee cup on a maintenance desk. Somewhere above him, metal bent with a long groan and then stopped. The men in the raptor yard had talked about the tyrannosaur the way people talked about a storm. Keep your distance. Watch the fences. Don’t assume because it is far away that it is not coming. Rex weather, one of them had said, and the others laughed because men laughed when a phrase was good enough to hide behind. Mateo understood it now. The tour road maintenance tunnel ran beneath a culvert and came up behind generator shed C. It was supposed to be dry except during seasonal service. It was not dry. Brown water moved against Mateo’s ankles and carried leaves, insulation, and one length of chain with a metal clip at the end. The chain had hair caught in it. Goat hair. Mateo stepped over it and did not look up at the ceiling. Looking up would not help. He followed the tunnel by touch. The lights failed in groups. Three bulbs on, two bulbs dead, one bulb buzzing like a trapped fly. Jophery’s route sheet said the tunnel door at the far end opened with permanent staff access during local safe mode. It did. Green. Green lied less underground. Maybe because the doors were older and expected less from people. Generator shed C was half flooded. The outside wall had cracked near the floor. Through the crack Mateo saw a section of fence lying flat in mud and rain. Beyond it, the tour road disappeared into trees. One of the yellow tour vehicles sat sideways across the lane with its roof crushed down at the back. The goat chain ended under it. Mateo found two dry road flares in a cabinet, a coil of rope, and a maintenance vest with reflective stripes. He put the vest on because he wanted to be seen by people and not seen b

title: Chapter 12: Utility Level B
text: Mateo got out through the floor. That was not a thing the safety signs admitted a person could do. The safety signs preferred stairs, muster points, and calm walking. The shed floor had a service hatch with two recessed bolts and a warning about confined spaces. The bolts were not tight. Nothing on the island was tight if a worker had needed to open it often enough. He used the wrench from the shuttle. Above him, the animal on the tour road breathed against the cracked wall. Mateo did not hurry with the second bolt. Hurrying stripped bolts. Stripped bolts made noise. Noise made teeth. The hatch opened into hot air. He climbed down a ladder into Utility Level B and pulled the hatch after him with one hand. The dark below smelled of old batteries, mineral water, and the green-black odor of drains that had been asked to do too much. Utility Level B was not a level. It was a mistake repeated under different buildings until someone gave it a name. Concrete corridors. Pipe runs. Cable trays. Floor channels. Little rooms with pumps, switches, and laminated instructions written for men who already knew what they were looking at. Mateo had cleaned hospitals older than this park. Hospitals hid their important work in back corridors too. Oxygen. Waste. Laundry. Bodies. The front of a place could lie only because the back kept working. He found a wall schematic under yellowed plastic. SERVICE SPINE B VISITOR CENTER / KITCHEN / CONTROL SUPPORT / NORTH MAINTENANCE Visitor center. Mateo touched the word with two wet fingers. If any rescue remained, it would go there. If any radio still reached the mainland, it would be near control. If the children on the wrong channel were real, the visitor center was where a child would run when adults told them a building was safe. He copied the schematic onto the back of the transport manifest with the grease pencil. The lines came out fat and ugly. Good. Pretty maps were for guests. Halfway down the spine, the lights changed color. Not off. B

title: Chapter 13: Kitchen Light
text: The door opened because a forgotten reader remembered him. Mateo stood with his hand still on his temporary badge and watched the green light as if it might change its mind. It did not. AIR HANDLER B-4 was loud, hot, and narrow. The floor was wet under the fan housings. The shortcut was supposed to be dry because dry meant no condensation spill, no stormwater backflow, no animal washdown contamination pushing the wrong direction through the service spine. The shortcut was wet. Mateo went through anyway. He had stopped believing in routes that were good. He had routes that existed. The air handler brought him behind the visitor center kitchen. He knew it before he saw the sign because the smell changed: cold meat, lemon cleaner, fryer oil gone stale, and the faint sweet smell of desserts left too long under glass. The kitchen lights were on. That was wrong enough to make him crouch. A safe building did not need every light. A building trying to convince people it was safe did. He opened the service panel and stepped into the back corridor behind the freezers. Stainless steel reflected the emergency bulbs in broken lines. A rack of clean pans had fallen across the floor. One pan rocked gently, not because anyone touched it, but because the refrigeration compressor below it kept kicking on and off. Mateo listened. No voices. No radio. No men pretending they were not afraid. He moved through the prep area with one hand on the counter. Flour stuck to the water on his sleeve. He almost laughed at that. Of all the things the island had put on him, flour was the most normal. There were prints on the floor. Small shoes. Two sets. Running. Mateo did not follow them into the dining room. He looked at the prints, looked at the swinging kitchen doors, and looked at Jophery’s sheet. Service route first. Guest route last. The park killed workers in the spaces between. He found a chef’s jacket and wrapped it around his left forearm. Not armor. Cloth. Cloth was better than skin. In

title: Chapter 14: Not My Rescue
text: Mateo did not run toward the guest stairs. He hated that later. At the time, he saw three things: the freezer reflection, the service door two meters behind him, and a white towel hanging from the cart handle. He pulled the towel loose, threw it at the freezer alarm panel, and hit the service door with his shoulder before the animal in the cold decided the beeping meant food. The guest stairs were on his right. Voices came from that direction. A girl. A boy. A man shouting one word Mateo could not make out. He had one second to turn. The service door was already closing behind him. Green did not mean open. Open did not mean enough time. The corridor beyond the kitchen ran past staff toilets and a janitor closet. Mateo stopped with his forehead against the painted block wall. He could still hear the freezer alarm through the door. Then the alarm cut off. Something hit the kitchen side of the door. Mateo kept moving. He told himself the children had adults. That the guest stairs led upward. That his route led to radios. That one more person in the kitchen would not make the children safer. All those things might have been true. They did not help. At the janitor closet, someone groaned. Mateo opened the door with the box cutter in his hand. Rosa was inside. He knew her voice first. The woman from the radio. East transport. Her hair was plastered against her face and one trouser leg was black with blood below the knee. She held a mop handle like a spear and almost put it through Mateo’s chest before she saw his face. “You,” she said. “Me.” “You missed it.” “I noticed.” Rosa gave one breath that was almost a laugh and then bent forward until her forehead touched the mop handle. She had stayed for a driver who never reached the dock. A gate stuck. A tire blew. The radio told her to shelter. The shelter door locked on the wrong side. Every part of her story had an instruction in it. Every instruction had failed by five minutes. Mateo wrapped her leg with the chef’s jacket.

title: Chapter 15: Compy Work
text: The little ones came through the trash room. Mateo heard them before he saw them. Not footsteps. Too light for that. Scratches. Plastic crackling. Wet snouts pushing through bags. The sound made the hair on his arms lift because it belonged to work, not monsters. Rats in hospital bins. Dogs behind markets. Animals that found what people threw away and made a living from it. Rosa leaned on the mop handle and whispered, “What is that?” “Garbage.” “Garbage does not breathe.” “This garbage does.” The helipad stairwell had taken them up one level and then stopped at a smoke door with no power. No red. No green. No light. The manual handle had been removed for construction and not replaced. Someone had taped over the empty screw holes as if tape could become metal if a supervisor did not look closely. They had gone back down. Now the service route forced them through waste sorting. Mateo hated that the little animals made sense there. Food scraps in white bags. Meat trimmings in red bags. Fruit rinds. Disposable cups. A whole tray of untouched dessert squares tipped into a bin because some guest-facing event had ended faster than planned. The back of the park had set a table for scavengers. One of the little animals climbed onto a bag and looked at him. It had bright eyes and a narrow head. It was not big. That made it worse. People respected big. People grew careless with small. “Do not kick them,” Mateo said. “I was not going to kick them.” “Everyone kicks small things when afraid.” He opened a cabinet and found powdered disinfectant, a spray wand, and a broken face shield. He poured disinfectant into a mop bucket and added water until the room stank sharp enough to make Rosa cough. Then he pushed the bucket ahead of them with the rolling cart. The little animals backed away from the smell. Not far. Far enough. Halfway across the room, Rosa slipped. Mateo grabbed her before she hit the floor. The mop handle clattered away. Every little head turned. Mateo threw the disin

title: Chapter 16: Quarantine Route
text: The quarantine route did not start with a door. It started with a smell. Not rot. Not meat. Not disinfectant. Something medicinal under wet fur, something Mateo knew from hospital cages where dogs woke badly from surgery. He stopped before the blue double doors and put his hand flat against the wall. Cold vibration. Fans were running on the other side. Rosa stood behind him with one shoulder against a pipe. Her face had gone the color of paper that had been wet and dried wrong. “Tell me this is not the only way,” she said. Mateo looked at the route sheet. The clean exterior route was crossed out. The helipad stair had no handle. The guest corridor had children, raptors, or both. The kitchen was no longer a kitchen in any useful sense. “It is not the only way,” he said. Rosa waited. “It is the shortest bad way.” Jophery had written one line beside the quarantine doors: NEVER ALONE HERE. Mateo showed it to her. “Good,” Rosa said. “Then you are following instructions.” Jophery’s badge opened the first door. The room beyond was a changing vestibule with hooks, masks, yellow boots, and a shower head hanging from the ceiling like a question. The second door had no reader. It had a wheel lock. Mateo turned it slowly. The wheel squealed. Everything behind the door stopped moving. That was worse than noise. They entered between plastic curtain strips gone cloudy from scratches. Empty cages lined the wall. Not large cages. Transport cages. Medical cages. Each had a clipboard. Most were blank. One had a tag with no animal name, only a number and a handwritten note: BITES LATCHES. Mateo did not like animals that learned hardware. The floor drain ran backward. Black water pushed up through the grate in little pulses. A feather floated in it. Then a second feather. Then a piece of gauze, pink at one end. “Keep your foot out of that,” he said. Rosa looked down and made a sound through her teeth. Halfway through the room, something tapped the inside of an empty cage. Mateo turned h

title: Chapter 17: Counting Error
text: Mateo held the vent grille in place with the mop handle while Rosa turned the screws back in by hand. The thing in the vent pushed once. Rosa said, “Do not let go.” “I had not planned to.” The screws were wet. Her fingers were wet. The animal behind the metal found the gap at the corner and put one claw through. The claw hooked the air, missed Mateo’s sleeve by two fingers, and vanished when Rosa got the last screw to bite. They did not wait to see if it held. The quarantine route let out behind the personnel accountability office. That name was printed on the door in calm black letters. Inside were two desks, one dead computer, one living terminal, and a wall board with magnetic names sorted into columns. ON SITE. EVACUATED. UNACCOUNTED. Mateo found Tomás under UNACCOUNTED. He found Rosa under EVACUATED. Rosa stared at her own name. “Efficient.” Mateo looked for himself. He was under EVACUATED too, but not printed. Pencil. Crossed once. Someone had written BOAT in small letters. He touched the mark. Then he looked for Jophery Brown. No magnet. No pencil. No dead column for men who had been eaten by a failure everyone signed. The living terminal blinked: ACCOUNTABILITY RECONCILIATION IN PROGRESS. Mateo pressed the arrow key. PERMANENT STAFF ACTIVE: BROWN, JOPHERY TEMPORARY CONTRACTORS REMAINING: 0 ACTION: CORRECT MISMATCH? No was not an option. Only ENTER. He did not press it. The terminal pressed itself after ten seconds. The printer began. CORRECTION APPLIED. VALVERDE, MATEO - DEPARTED EAST DOCK. BROWN, JOPHERY - ACTIVE SERVICE RESPONSE. Mateo tore the paper so hard it ripped through the line with his name. Rosa did not tell him to calm down. That made him trust her more. “We can take the board,” she said. “What?” “The magnets. The paper. Your crossed-out name.” “For who?” “For whoever tries to say you were never here.” He put the torn reconciliation page in the plastic sleeve with Jophery’s complaint. The terminal chimed. NEW SERVICE RESPONSE ASSIGNED. RAPTOR SER

title: Chapter 18: Raptor Silence
text: The raptor service corridor had gone quiet. Mateo had never heard it quiet before. Even empty, the place had made noises. Fans. Drains. distant relays. Men coughing into radios. The raptors themselves, sometimes, a bark or scrape from behind layers of steel and mesh. Workplaces had sounds. Sounds told you which machines still cared. Now there was only water. Drip from the ceiling. Runoff under the door. Rosa breathing through pain beside him. Mateo held Jophery’s badge away from his chest so it would not tap the plastic sleeve with every breath. He had begun to hate that little sound. It announced him to machines. It announced a dead man to doors. D-17 stood at the end of the hall. The reader with the bad complaint. The reader Jophery had reported. The reader that might open to Tomás or to nothing a person should enter. “We do this fast,” Rosa whispered. “Fast breaks things.” “Slow gets eaten.” Both true. They moved. The first raptor door was open. Not wide. Not broken. Open three inches, held by the automatic latch as if someone had asked politely. Mateo shone the flashlight through the gap. Empty holding room. Straw. Scrape marks. A chain hanging from the wall. The second door was open too. So was the third. No alarms. No red lights. Quiet had a shape now. It was every door that should have been closed. At D-17, the reader screen was black. Mateo saw his own face in it. Wet hair. Split lip he did not remember getting. One eye wide enough to look foolish. Behind his reflection, a second shape lifted its head. He did not turn. Turning took time. He grabbed Rosa and shoved her into the wall recess beside the reader as the raptor struck the corridor where her body had been. The animal hit tile and corrected without slipping. That was the terrible part. Everything else slipped here. Men. carts. water. Mateo. The raptor’s feet found the floor like it had read it better than he ever would. Mateo slapped Jophery’s badge against D-17. Nothing. The animal lowered its head.

title: Chapter 19: Full Reboot
text: The door opened inward. Mateo fell through it because he had been leaning into the reader, expecting failure. Rosa fell after him. The raptor’s jaw closed on the mop handle instead of her leg. Wood cracked. Rosa let go. The door shut. For one second the raptor was on the other side and they were alive. Then every light in the room went white. Not emergency white. Full white. Office white. Visitor-center white. The white of systems pretending nothing bad had happened because electricity had returned and electricity made cowards confident. A chime sounded through the room. FULL SYSTEM REBOOT COMPLETE. The speaker voice was cheerful. Mateo hated it more than the alarms. Rosa lay on her side, panting. “Tell me that helps.” Mateo looked at the reader inside the room. Jophery’s badge: red. His temporary badge: red. He tried Jophery’s again. Red. The display read: INVALID - DECEASED EMPLOYEE. Mateo stared at it. Rosa said, “They fixed it.” “No.” “What do you mean no?” “They fixed the dead man.” He put his temporary badge flat to the reader. This time the display did not reject it. It searched. Long enough for the raptor outside to put one claw into the gap below the door and pull. The display changed. TEMP PROFILE CACHE FOUND. Mateo stopped breathing. VALID FOR LOCAL EXIT ONLY. VALVERDE, MATEO. Green. He hit the open button. The wrong door opened. Not the corridor door. A rear service hatch behind them. The system had given Mateo what he was allowed to have, not what he wanted. Local exit. One way. No choice. He dragged Rosa through the hatch as the raptor peeled metal at the corridor door. The hatch dropped them into a narrow records room behind raptor service. Metal shelves. Old forms. Dead monitors. Boxes marked INCIDENT VIDEO - DO NOT DUPLICATE. One monitor woke when the reboot reached it. It showed a map. Two dots. VALVERDE, M. - LOCAL EXIT BROWN, J. - DECEASED Then the map zoomed. TRANSMITTING LOCATION TO CENTRAL SECURITY. For the first time all night, the park knew

title: Chapter 20: After the Click
text: After the click, the park woke in pieces. Not the right pieces. Fans came on in rooms with open doors. Locks cycled in empty halls. Freezer alarms reset. A phone rang under a desk no one could reach. Somewhere above raptor service, sprinklers began spraying water over concrete already wet enough to shine. The records-room monitor kept transmitting Mateo’s location. Rosa smashed it with the tape box. The screen died. The little green transmit light on the CPU stayed on. “That did not work,” Mateo said. “I noticed.” He yanked the power cord. The green light died. From the corridor outside came the clean sound of a lock opening. Rosa looked at him. Mateo looked at the only other door. It had a manual latch and a deadbolt. The latch opened. The deadbolt did not. He used the wrench. The wrench slipped and took skin from his knuckles. He did not feel it until blood made the handle slick. “Mateo.” “I know.” “Mateo.” “I know.” The deadbolt gave. They went through into a hall lined with staff lockers. Halfway down, a man sat against the wall with a radio in his lap. Tomás Araya. Mateo stopped so hard Rosa hit his back. Tomás opened one eye. “Temporary,” he said. Mateo dropped beside him. Tomás had a bite high on one arm and a bad cut across his side where something metal, not teeth, had opened him. He had tied a belt around the arm. The belt was wrong. Too low. Too loose. Work done one-handed in the dark. “Can you stand?” Mateo asked. “No.” “We can carry–“ “No.” Tomás looked at Rosa. “You are supposed to be on a boat.” “Everyone keeps telling me.” The corridor lock behind them clicked again. Tomás pushed his permanent badge into Mateo’s hand. “No.” “Yes.” “We have Jophery’s.” “Dead now. System knows. Mine still has animal-service override.” “Then we take you.” Tomás grabbed Mateo’s sleeve with his good hand. His grip was weak. That made it worse. “You take the living woman who can walk badly. You take the papers. You tell my sister I stayed mean to the end.” “No.” Tomás smil

title: Chapter 21: Kitchen Without Children
text: The kitchen had no children in it. That was the first mercy. It had everything children left behind. Muddy prints on tile. A ladle under the stove line. A cabinet door hanging crooked. A pan dented where something had stepped on it. On the stainless counter, one small handprint in condensation, already fading because the freezer air had begun to warm. Mateo looked at the handprint too long. Rosa touched his arm. “They got out.” “You know?” “No.” It was an honest answer, which meant it helped. They had come back through the kitchen because Tomás’s badge opened animal-service doors but not guest stairwells, and because the service spine to North Maintenance ran under the dish pit. Mateo did not think about Tomás after that sentence. He put the thought on a shelf. The shelf was wet. The shelf would break later. For now, kitchen. He read it like work. Freezer door scraped at bottom: opened hard, then blocked. Shelf knocked down but not collapsed: animal weight near edge, not full body. Food torn, not eaten: distraction. Water under dishwasher moving toward the wrong drain: storm line failing. The raptors had learned the room faster than the people who built the room. Rosa leaned on the prep counter. “What are you doing?” “Trying not to step where they stepped.” “Why?” “Because they did not slip.” The freezer bumped once. Both of them looked. The walk-in door was closed from the outside with a serving cart wedged under the handle. Frost melted down the seam. Rosa lifted the mop handle she had taken from Tomás’s hall. “Do we open it?” Mateo listened. Another bump. Weak. Not claws. Knuckles. He moved the cart. A man fell out with the cold. Not a guest. Not security. Kitchen whites under a rain jacket. His lips were blue and one eyebrow had frozen stiff with blood. He blinked at Mateo and tried to say something, but his jaw shook too hard. Rosa and Mateo dragged him behind the counter. “Name,” Rosa said. “Luis,” the man managed. “Can you walk?” He laughed once, which became

title: Chapter 22: North Dock Promise
text: North Dock was not on the guest map. It was on three bad maps and one good rumor. Jophery’s route sheet showed a service road broken by a creek crossing. Tomás’s badge opened animal-service gates along the first half. Luis knew the fuel locker code because kitchen deliveries used the north route when investors wanted the east dock to look clean. Rosa knew the radio phrase for a maintenance launch because she had signed boats out twice and been yelled at both times for writing too slowly. Between them, they had almost enough. Almost was a cruel word. Luis could walk if Mateo took most of his weight. Rosa could walk if she hated the floor enough. Mateo could walk because nobody had asked his body permission. They left the kitchen through the dish pit and came out under the visitor center loading dock. Dawn had not arrived, but the night had thinned. Rain still fell. The big storm was moving off or saving strength. Mateo did not trust either possibility. The North Dock route began behind a gate marked MAINTENANCE ONLY. Tomás’s badge opened it. Mateo did not thank the machine. The service road beyond was narrow, cut through wet green walls. Twice they had to stop and listen. Once, a branch moved against the wind. Once, nothing moved at all and that was enough to make them crouch behind a utility cart until Luis started shaking from cold. At the creek crossing, the road was gone. Not flooded. Gone. Water had eaten the edge and dropped half the concrete into the channel. On the other side, the road continued as if nothing had happened because roads were stupid that way. Mateo found the old pipe bridge under the crossing. It was not meant for people. It was three pipes strapped to steel brackets, wet with spray, running over brown water full of branches. Jophery had drawn it as a dotted line and written: IF YOU ARE SMALL AND STUPID. “Good news,” Mateo said. Rosa looked at the pipes. “No.” “It is on the map.” “That is not an argument.” Luis laughed, then coughed until he ha

title: Chapter 23: The Wrong Dead Man
text: Mateo knew the right answer. That was the problem. He looked at Rosa. She had one hand on the rope around Luis and one hand on the pipe rail. She heard the voice. She heard the question. She also saw the badges on Mateo’s lanyard: green temporary plastic, Jophery’s cloudy permanent card, Tomás’s scratched animal-service override. Say name. Mateo pressed the handset. “Mateo Valverde.” Static. The man’s voice came back flatter. “No active Valverde on island manifest.” “The manifest is wrong.” “Identify by active credential.” Mateo closed his eyes. Rosa said, “Mateo.” Not warning. Not permission. Just his name, so one human being had said it before the machine took it. He pressed the button again. “Jophery Brown.” The radio clicked into a different channel at once. “Brown, you are listed deceased.” “Then your list is half right.” Silence. Luis whispered, “Jesus.” The man on the radio said, “How many with you?” “Three. One injured transport. One kitchen staff hypothermic. One temporary contractor.” “Temporary contractor does not show.” “I know.” “North Dock launch has fuel for one crossing. Gate is locked under animal-service control. Approach lights are off. We can open for Brown.” Mateo looked at Tomás’s badge. “Open for Brown,” he said. The words tasted bad. The gate on the far side of the road clicked so loudly they heard it through the rain. They moved faster after that because shame was also a kind of fuel. The road narrowed. The jungle pressed close. Once, Rosa stopped and pointed to mud where three-toed tracks crossed the road and vanished into ferns. The tracks were not huge. They did not need to be. At the final bend, they saw North Dock lights. Two of them. Low yellow bulbs over a fuel shed. Not enough light for rescue. Enough for bait. The launch sat in black water beside the dock, small, white, and real. A man in a poncho stood near it with a shotgun pointed at the ground. He raised the gun when he saw them. “Brown?” Mateo stepped forward. “No.” The man’s f

title: Chapter 24: Using the Park
text: Mateo stopped being polite to the park. That was the difference. Before North Dock, he had asked doors to open. He had asked radios to believe him. He had asked maps to be right and badges to mean what the plastic said. At North Dock, with a shotgun pointed at his chest and the jungle making raptor sounds behind the fuel shed, he stopped asking. He used what was there. The man with the shotgun was named Pike. He worked maintenance launch. He had been told to wait for Brown. He had not been told Brown was dead because InGen information moved like a drain full of hair: slowly, badly, and only after pressure built enough to flood the floor. “Put the gun down,” Rosa said. Pike did not. Mateo looked past him. Fuel shed. Hose reel. Bait freezer for dock scavenger control. Laundry cart with canvas sides. Gate reader. Manual winch. Emergency horn. All things workers used when nobody important watched. The raptor bark came again. Pike turned his head. Mateo moved. He did not hit Pike. He hit the emergency horn. The dock screamed. Every animal in the trees turned toward the sound. Pike fired into leaves. Rosa shoved Luis behind the bait freezer. Mateo grabbed the fuel hose, opened the valve, and sprayed diesel across the concrete between the gate and the shed. “Are you insane?” Pike shouted. “No.” That was not fully true. Mateo threw one of the road flares. The diesel did not explode. Movies lied. It burned ugly and low, a dirty orange line that made heat shimmer over the wet concrete and put a smell in the air so strong even the rain could not flatten it. The raptor came to the edge of the light and stopped. Not afraid. Calculating. Mateo used Tomás’s badge on the bait freezer. Green. He opened it and almost vomited from the smell. Old meat. Fish heads. Something labeled GOAT PARTS - DO NOT USE GUEST CART. He shoved the bait bin onto the laundry cart. “Push,” he told Pike. “What?” “Push the cart.” “To where?” Mateo pointed at the gate reader. Pike looked at the fire. Then at

title: Chapter 25: Left Behind
text: The launch access reader did not care that Mateo was alive. Red. He tried Tomás’s badge. Red. Jophery’s. Red. Three cards. Three little deaths. Pike shouted something from the dock. Rosa shouted back. Luis was coughing behind the fuel shed. The second raptor stood between the people and the boat with its head low and its tail held still. The fire made orange lines along its wet back. Mateo looked at the reader. Not the light. The screws. Four screws. Two rusted. One missing. One replaced with the wrong size. He laughed then, one short ugly sound, because of course. The park had not been built by miracles. It had been built by people who lost screws and covered the hole with tape. It had been maintained by men like Jophery and Tomás, who wrote warnings no one read. It could be opened the same way. He used the box cutter on the reader seal and the wrench on the wrong screw. The panel came loose enough to show wires. Red, black, yellow, green. He had seen the same colors in the hospital laundry lift. He touched red to green. The launch gate opened. Pike fired once. The raptor snapped sideways, not hit, only annoyed. Rosa threw the mop handle. It struck the fuel shed wall. The animal turned toward that smaller insult. “Now,” Mateo said. Everyone moved. Luis fell into the boat more than climbed. Rosa followed. Pike jumped down and started the engine with shaking hands. Mateo stayed at the reader with the wires pinched together until the gate finished its slow travel. The raptor saw him. For one second Mateo saw himself in its eye: wet, small, temporary, holding two wires because the official way had failed. Then the launch engine caught. Rosa reached for him. Mateo let go of the wires and ran. He made three steps before the raptor crossed the dock. Not enough steps. Rosa screamed his name. That was the important part.
